claims.claim: Cells subjected to low-temperature cycling (at -5°C) demonstrated a self-heating onset temperature decrease from approximately 102°C to 82°C, a difference of approximately 20 degrees, compared to fresh cells.
claims.quote: the self-heating onset temperature was lower for cells cycled at temperatures of −5°C, showing a decrease from 102°C to 82°C, a difference of approximately 20 degrees.

claims.claim: Cells cycled at -5°C showed minimal changes in both ohmic and charge transfer resistance with no obvious dual semicircles in EIS, indicating that the predominant aging mechanism at low temperatures is lithium plating rather than SEI thickening.
claims.quote: cells cycled at −5°C showed minimal changes in both ohmic and charge transfer resistance, with no obvious dual semicircles, indicating the predominant aging mechanism at low temperatures is lithium plating.

claims.claim: Cells aged through low-temperature cycling exhibit a substantial decrease in activation energy for thermal runaway compared to fresh cells and cells cycled at 25°C, and this lower activation energy aligns with their earlier entry into self-heating mode.
claims.quote: cells aged through low-temperature cycling exhibit a substantial decrease in activation energy compared to other groups. This aligns with the observation that cells undergoing low-temperature aging enter self-heating mode earlier.

claims.claim: Despite all aged cell groups showing similar state of health (SOH) of approximately 80%, cells subjected to low-temperature cycling demonstrated a considerably lower onset temperature for exothermic reactions and shorter thermal runaway delay time, demonstrating that SOH alone is insufficient to characterize thermal runaway risk of aged cells.
claims.quote: cells subjected to low-temperature cycling demonstrated a considerably lower onset temperature for exothermic reactions, and shorter TR delay time, despite all aged cells showing similar state of health (SOH)... demonstrates the insufficiency of traditionally defined SOH in the characterization of thermal runaway of aged cells.

claims.claim: Cells from a used Dyson vacuum cleaner (dynamic cycling with calendar aging) exhibited voltage drops at relatively lower temperatures compared to other aging conditions, indicating greater sensitivity to internal short circuit triggers.
claims.quote: in the case of aged cell from a used Dyson vacuum cleaner, where the voltage drops first at relatively low temperatures compared to other aging conditions. This phenomenon suggests that these cells with dynamic cycling with calendar aging are more sensitive to internal short circuit triggers.

claims.claim: Mass loss during thermal runaway ranged from 22% to 29% across all cell conditions, with fresh cells exhibiting higher total mass loss than aged cells, attributed to capacity degradation and loss of active materials in aged cells reducing thermal runaway intensity.
claims.quote: The mass loss during the TR tests ranges from 22% to 29% for the cells, with a higher total mass loss observed in fresh cells. This phenomenon can be attributed to the degradation of cell capacity and the loss of active materials in aged cells, leading to a decrease in thermal runaway intensity and less materials being ejected during TR.

claims.claim: Cells aged through cycling at 25°C showed activation energy and frequency factor values nearly identical to those of fresh cells, indicating minimal differences in thermal runaway kinetics between ambient-temperature cycled and fresh cells.
claims.quote: the activation energy and frequency factor of cells aged through cycling at 25°C are nearly identical to those of fresh cells, indicating minimal differences.

claims.claim: Charging current has only minor influence on battery overcharge behaviors: TTR remains at around 113 °C and SOCTR decreases only slightly from 1.674 to 1.652 when charging rate rises from 1/3C to 1C under adiabatic condition.
claims.quote: the three batteries show similar temperature profiles and run into TR at around 113 °C, while the temperature rate increases with the rise of charging current... Consequently, SOCTR decreases slightly from 1.674 to 1.652 and Tmax increases from 779.6 °C to 816.0 °C when the charging rate rises from 1/3C to 1C.

claims.claim: A battery with restraining plate and cuts on pouches (configuration C) under adiabatic condition achieves SOCTR of 1.738 and TTR of 140.9 °C, compared to SOCTR of 1.670 and TTR of 113.1 °C for the battery without restraining plate (configuration A), demonstrating significantly improved overcharge performance.
claims.quote: The battery with restraining plate and cuts on the pouches (configuration C) presents the best overcharge performance with SOCTR rising to 1.738 and TTR to 140.9 °C, though the voltage and resistance profiles remain almost unchanged.

claims.claim: All overcharged cathodes show no obvious exothermic behaviors until temperature reaches around 250 °C, which is much higher than the TTR (less than 150 °C) observed in all seven overcharge tests, indicating that cathode decomposition contributes little to battery heat generation before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: all the overcharged cathodes show no obvious exothermic behaviors until temperature reaches around 250 °C, much higher than the TTR (less than 150 °C) in the seven overcharge tests, indicating that decomposition of cathode material contributes little to battery heat generation until TR happens.

claims.claim: Lithium metal begins to deposit on the anode surface when SOC exceeds 1.25, and the total anode heat generation increases to more than 1.5 times its initial value by SOC 1.85 (from 1592 J/g at 1.00 SOC to 2553 J/g at 1.85 SOC), indicating that deposited lithium metal accelerates battery temperature rise.
claims.quote: lithium metal begins to deposit on anode surface when SOC exceeds 1.25... the total heat generation of the anode also increases significantly and finally reaches more than 1.5 times of its initial value, indicating that the deposited lithium metal on anode surface is more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperature and would accelerate the temperature increase of the battery.

claims.claim: For batteries with configuration C (restraining plate and cuts on pouches), thermal runaway initiates at approximately 141 °C, coinciding with the melting temperature of the PE separator, because separator shrinkage causes direct cathode-anode contact leading to internal short circuit.
claims.quote: The battery temperature rate keeps increasing as the temperature rises from 110 °C to 140 °C, and TR happens at around 141 °C, the same as the melting temperature of PE in separator measured by DSC... Once the separator starts to shrink due to PE melting, the cathode will directly contact with anode and internal short circuit occurs, generating a large amount of ohmic heat and finally triggering TR.

claims.claim: For batteries without pressure relief (configurations A and B), thermal runaway is initiated by pouch rupture and ejection of high-pressure gas, which causes sparks that ignite flammable gas (DMC, EMC, CO, CH4), whereas batteries with configuration C show no obvious swelling and exhibit a different TR initiation mechanism via separator melting.
claims.quote: Ruptures of the pouches and ejection of high-pressure gas occur prior to TR, resulting in sharp decrease of the temperature rate... Sparks are then observed on the edge of the battery... The sparks will ignite the flammable gas (DMC, EMC, CO, CH4, etc.), and then trigger more exothermic reactions, leading to TR.

claims.claim: Under non-adiabatic condition, the battery with configuration C achieves SOCTR of 1.854 compared to 1.738 under adiabatic condition, demonstrating that improved heat dissipation significantly enhances overcharge performance especially when SOC exceeds 1.6.
claims.quote: The battery with configuration C shows a significant increase of SOCTR (from 1.738 to 1.854) under non-adiabatic condition compared to that under adiabatic condition... indicating that excellent heat dissipation can help to lower the heat accumulation within the battery and thus improve the battery overcharge performance.

claims.claim: In a 18650 NCA cell heated at 5000 W/m², the safety order of heating positions from most to least safe is: vertical 10 mm > bottom end > bottom 5 mm > centre 10 mm > bottom 10 mm.
claims.quote: Considering the time taken to failure state, the safety order for the current analysis is shown as follows: vertical 10 mm > bottom end > bottom 5 mm > centre 10 mm > bottom 10 mm.

claims.claim: At 5000 W/m² heating power, the maximum temperature attained with center 10 mm heating is higher than all other heating positions by 60–70 K.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature attained by center 10 mm heating is higher than the remaining heating positions by 60–70 K.

claims.claim: The negative solvent reaction releases the maximum heat during thermal runaway of a 18650 cell, releasing 1984 W, which is 13 times more than the heat released during electrolyte breakdown (148 W).
claims.quote: During the thermal runaway failure, negative solvent reaction released a heat of 1984 W, 1157 W during internal short circuit, 479 W during positive solvent decomposition reaction, and 148 W during electrolyte break down. The heat released by negative solvent reaction is 13 times more than heat released during electrolyte break down reaction.

claims.claim: In a 3×3 battery pack with side cell heating, increasing spacing from 2 mm to 8 mm reduces thermal runaway time non-uniformly, with the maximum reduction of 85 s occurring between 6 mm and 8 mm spacing.
claims.quote: With increase in spacing, a reduction in thermal runaway time was observed from 2 mm to 8 mm. But the reduction is not uniform with increase of spacing width. The maximum reduction time of 85 s was observed between 6 mm to 8 mm.

claims.claim: In a 3×3 battery pack with center cell heating, increasing spacing from 4 mm to 6 mm results in a 50 s smaller delay increment compared to the 150 s delay gained by increasing spacing from 2 mm to 4 mm, and a drop in maximum temperature of 30 K.
claims.quote: There is a delay of 150 s with an increase in spacing from 2 mm to 4 mm. When the spacing increased from 4 mm to 6 mm, the increase in delay reduced by 50 s and there was a drop in maximum temperature by 30 K.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature attained by the 7000 W/m² heating power battery pack is greater than that of the 10,000 W/m² battery pack by 10 K, while increasing power from 7000 W/m² to 10,000 W/m² reduces time to thermal runaway by 17%.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature obtained by 7000 W/m2 heating power is found to be slightly higher than that of 10,000 W/m2 by 10 K. The time gap between failure of row2 with two different heating powers was found to be 60 s, which indicated a decrease in time delay by 17 % less than the 7000 W/m2 heating power.

claims.claim: The peak temperature of LFP cathode material during thermal runaway is approximately 25% less than that of NCA type cathode material under bottom heating conditions.
claims.quote: The peak temperature attained by LFP cathode material is almost 25 % less than that of NCA type cathode material.

claims.claim: In a 3×3 battery pack at 7000 W/m², the entire 9-cell pack enters thermal runaway within 25–30 s after the initial trigger in the first cell when spacing is 6 mm.
claims.quote: The entire 9 cell battery pack went thermal runaway within 25–30 s after the initial trigger of thermal runaway in a cell.

claims.claim: The direction of coolant airflow determines the thermal runaway spreading pattern in a battery pack, with the last row (downstream) failing first when the center cell is heated.
claims.quote: The last row cells failed quickly compared to the first row because the flow direction of air is from first row to last row.

claims.claim: For a 18650 cell with LCO cathode heated at the bottom, doubling the heating power from 2000 W/m² to 4000 W/m² decreases thermal runaway time by 332 s, while a further 1.25× increase to 5000 W/m² decreases failure time by only 25 s.
claims.quote: There was a decrease in thermal runaway time by 332 s when the heating power is doubled, when it is further increased by 1.25 times the decrease in failure time was observed to be 25 s.

claims.claim: During the 0.5C overcharge test, the expansion force peaked at 13918.9 N before venting, while the battery surface temperature remained consistently below 50°C prior to the peak.
claims.quote: This force peaked at 13918.9 N before venting, a value high enough to potentially compromise the structural integrity of the electrical system. Notably, before the expansion force reached its peak, the battery surface temperature remained consistently below 50 ◦C, and the voltage remained relatively stable.

claims.claim: At the time of safety valve opening, the combined strengthening effects (strain hardening contributing 64.67 MPa and strain rate hardening contributing 22.15 MPa) exceeded thermal softening (18.41 MPa reduction) by 371.59%.
claims.quote: By the time the safety valve activated, strain hardening had contributed 64.67 MPa to the increase in yield strength, and strain rate hardening had contributed an additional 22.15 MPa. Conversely, thermal softening induces a strength reduction of 18.41 MPa. At this point, the total strengthening effect is 371.59% greater than the weakening effect from thermal softening.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway, thermal softening (151.35 MPa reduction) exceeds the combined strengthening from strain hardening (78.93 MPa) and strain rate hardening (25.34 MPa) by 45.15%, reversing the pre-venting relationship.
claims.quote: When the yield strength reaches its minimum point after TR, the strengthening from strain hardening is 78.93 MPa, and that from the strain rate hardening is 25.34 MPa, while the reduction from thermal softening reaches 151.35 MPa. At this point, the weakening effect exceeds the combined strengthening effects by 45.15%.

claims.claim: Under uniaxial constraint conditions, suppression of side-wall deformation forces the top and bottom plates to bulge outward, concentrating maximum plastic strain and energy dissipation at geometric discontinuities near the corners.
claims.quote: Simulation results show that under typical uniaxial constraints, the suppression of side-wall deformation driven by coupled internal pressure and thermal loads forces the top and bottom plates to bulge outward. This deformation pattern concentrates stress at geometric discontinuities near the corners, resulting in maximum plastic strain and energy dissipation.

claims.claim: Increasing the top plate thickness from 1.5 mm to 3.0 mm (with side-wall thickness fixed at 1.5 mm) reduces the maximum plastic strain in the critical corner region by 40.0% and reduces the top-surface PEEQ from 3.3% to 1.6% (a 51.5% reduction) after 0.5C overcharge.
claims.quote: Specifically, as the top cap thickness increases from 1.5 mm to 3.0 mm, the PEEQ in the corner region decreases from 10.5% to 6.3%, a reduction of 40.0%. Concurrently, as the PEEQ of the top surface decreases from 3.3% to 1.6%, a reduction of 51.5% occurs.

claims.claim: Increasing only the side-wall thickness results in nearly identical maximum PEEQ values across all three casing regions, providing negligible protection to critical corner regions because bending stiffness is proportional to the cube of thickness.
claims.quote: In contrast, simply increasing the side-wall thickness results in nearly identical maximum PEEQ values in all three regions... Thus, thickening the top and bottom plates is far more effective at reducing corner deformation than increasing the side wall thickness, which offers negligible protection to the critical corner regions.

claims.claim: Stage V of the overcharge process, triggered by internal short circuit onset, produced a second peak in expansion force and a maximum temperature of approximately 475°C.
claims.quote: The onset of internal short circuit (ISC) triggered a massive release of thermal energy and a cascade of side reactions, which led to a second peak in expansion force and a maximum temperature of approximately 475 ℃.

claims.claim: In NCM811|Gr pouch cells charged to 4.2 V, the base electrolyte triggers thermal runaway at T2 = 223.6 °C with a maximum temperature T3 = 715.4 °C, while the high safety electrolyte (HSE) raises T2 to 248.0 °C (increase of 24.4 °C) and reduces T3 to 537.7 °C (reduction of 177.7 °C).
claims.quote: the thermal runaway with base electrolyte (BE) is triggered at 223.6 °C (T2) and the maximum temperature reaches 715.4 °C (T3). By sharply contrast, with the TR pathway restriction of HSE, T2 is increased to 248.0 °C that is 24.4 °C higher, while T3 drops to 537.7 °C which is significantly reduced by 177.7 °C at 4.2 V state.

claims.claim: When the cut-off voltage is increased from 4.2 V to 4.5 V, the thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 with base electrolyte decreases by 29 °C (from 223.6 °C to 194.6 °C), while T2 with HSE remains almost unchanged (from 248.0 °C to 245.0 °C).
claims.quote: From 4.2 to 4.5 V, the T2 with BE decreases by 29 °C (223.6 to 194.6 °C) while HSE-based cell remains almost unchanged (248.0 to 245.0 °C).

claims.claim: The thermal runaway incubation time with HSE extends from 8.03 × 10^4 s to 9.25 × 10^4 s compared to base electrolyte, demonstrating postponement of thermal decomposition.
claims.quote: the incubation time (Δt) of TR extends from 8.03 × 10^4 s to 9.25 × 10^4 s, demonstrating the postponement of thermal decomposition in battery.

claims.claim: The DSC peak exothermic temperature of the mixture of fully charged anode and HSE is 197.1 °C, compared to 130.9 °C for the base electrolyte, mitigating heat accumulation at the early stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The exothermic profile of the mixture of the anode and HSE demonstrates the peak temperature at 197.1 °C compared with BE at 130.9 °C, mitigating the heat accumulation at an early stage of TR.

claims.claim: The self-extinguishing time (SET) of the base electrolyte is 29.3 s g⁻¹, while the HSE has a SET of zero, indicating completely non-flammable behavior attributed to fluorine- and P-containing cyclophosphazene capturing H• radicals.
claims.quote: The SET value of BE is 29.3 s g⁻¹ while that of HSE is zero (Fig. 2c), exhibiting the property of non-flammable thoroughly, which is ascribed to the enrichment of fluorine- and P-containing cyclophosphazene to catch H•, then quenching the following reactions and stopping the flame propagation.

claims.claim: In a 9-cell battery module simulation, base electrolyte cells show thermal runaway propagation at 29 s per cell with a maximum module temperature of 730.6 °C, whereas HSE-based cells self-quench after only three cells trigger thermal runaway with a maximum temperature of 202.7 °C.
claims.quote: The spreading time of the 9-cell with BE is as low as 29 s per cell, and the maximum temperature of the module is as high as 730.6 °C. In sharply comparison, the HSE based cells do not suffer TR of the whole module, only three cells triggered TR with a maximum temperature of 202.7 °C in cell 4, and quenched from 222 s.

claims.claim: At a cut-off voltage of 4.5 V after 200 cycles, NCM811|Gr pouch cells with HSE retain 97.05% capacity with average Coulombic efficiency above 99.94%, compared to 53.85% capacity retention for cells with base electrolyte.
claims.quote: At 4.5 V condition, the capacity retention of HSE exhibits 97.05% after 200 cycles with an average Coulombic efficiency (CE) above 99.94% in comparison to the BE with the capacity retention of 53.85%.

claims.claim: Reductive gas released by the lithiated anode can migrate through holes in a ceramic-coated separator to attack the cathode, demonstrating that a physical separator barrier alone cannot prevent thermal runaway via chemical crosstalk.
claims.quote: The direct contact between cathode and anode has been cut-off through ceramic-coating on the separator... However, TR still occurs because oxide/reductive species can migrate through the holes of the separator.

claims.claim: PO• radicals emitted from the pyrolysis of PFPN capture free radicals H• and OH•, impeding combustion chain reactions and reducing heat release during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the PO• emitted from the pyrolysis of PFPN helps capture the free radicals, which impedes the chain reactions to reduce the heat release effectively.

claims.claim: The HSE-derived CEI layer is approximately 3 nm thick and is composed of N, P, and F elements, with low organic content and high inorganic species content compared to base electrolyte-derived CEI.
claims.quote: a uniform CEI layer (~3 nm in thickness) is closely attached to the cathode particles. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) confirms that the components of CEI layer are composed of N, P, and F elements... the CEI layer in HSE has low contents of organic components and high intensity of inorganic species.

claims.claim: Cycling at 45°C leads to the formation of an effective SEI, while cycling at 20°C leads to the formation of a thick decomposition layer on the anode surface.
claims.quote: the formation of an effective solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) during cycling at 45°C and a thick decomposition layer on the anode surface at 20°C

claims.claim: Cells cycled at 45°C achieved more than 1100 cycles to an SOH of 70%, while cells cycled at 20°C showed severe capacity fading after about 100 cycles and less than 400 cycles to reach an SOH of 70%.
claims.quote: The good electrochemical performance of the cells cycled at 45°C (more than 1100 cycles to an SOH of 70% [2]) is reflected by the marginal changes of the surface morphology... the cells cycled at 20°C showed a poor electrochemical performance with severe capacity fading after about 100 cycles (less than 400 cycles to an SOH of 70% [2])

claims.claim: The thermal breakdown of the SEI was reported to occur at about 100°C, leading to low heat generation due to decomposition of thermally unstable organic components and conversion to a more thermally stable SEI.
claims.quote: The thermal breakdown of the SEI was reported to occur at about 100°C [1,17–20], leading to a low heat generation due to the decomposition of the thermally unstable organic components and the conversion to a more thermally stable SEI

claims.claim: The progressive thermal decomposition of the cathode starting at about 200°C leads to oxygen evolution that initiates exothermic reactions including the oxidation of the electrolyte.
claims.quote: The progressive thermal decomposition of the cathode starting at about 200°C leads to oxygen evolution that initiates a series of exothermic reactions like the oxidation of the electrolyte

claims.claim: Cells in the fully discharged state (SOC 0%) showed no thermal runaway within the measured temperature range below 300°C, with the thermal runaway temperature estimated at TTR(SOC 0%) > 300°C.
claims.quote: the discharged cells (SOC 0%) that showed no thermal runaway in the measured temperature range (<300°C). Since the largest self-heating rates in this temperature range are below 1 K/min (see Fig. 5), the thermal runaway is supposed to occur at TTR(SOC 0%) > 300°C.

claims.claim: Metallic lithium deposits on the anode cycled at 20°C caused an earlier onset of exothermic reactions and a less pronounced correlation between onset temperature and SOC compared to fresh cells.
claims.quote: The aged cells exhibit an overall earlier onset of exothermic reactions and a less pronounced correlation with the SOC. The onset at lower temperatures was attributed to the presence of metallic lithium in the thick decomposition layer as determined by 7Li MAS NMR studies.

claims.claim: The thermally stable SEI formed during cycling at 45°C impedes the de-intercalation of lithium ions, resulting in a higher onset temperature for exothermic reactions averaging 99°C across all SOHs tested.
claims.quote: the high stability of the SEI formed at 45°C leads to a delayed thermally induced de-intercalation of lithium ions and, therefore, to a higher onset temperature. The rise of Tonset to an average of 99°C for all SOHs emphasizes the influence of the SEI on the thermal stability and the safety of LIBs.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature for fresh and aged fully charged cells was in a narrow range of 214°C ≤ TTR ≤ 221°C, indicating that the specific SOH and cycling conditions tested did not significantly affect the thermal runaway temperature.
claims.quote: the TTR temperatures are in a narrow range of 214°C ≤ TTR ≤ 221°C

claims.claim: The onset of exothermic reactions is determined by the anode side, while the thermal runaway is dominated by the decomposition of the cathode and its subsequent reactions with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: it can be stated that the onset of exothermic reactions is determined by the anode and the intercalated Li-ions or deposited 'mossy' lithium, while the thermal runaway is dominated by the reactivity of the cathode with the electrolyte.

claims.claim: A pronounced 7Li MAS NMR signal at 262 ppm was detected for the anode cycled at 20°C, indicating deposition of metallic ('mossy') lithium on the anode surface, while this signal was absent in the anode cycled at 45°C.
claims.quote: a pronounced signal at 262 ppm was detected for the anode aged at 20°C indicating a deposition of metallic (rather 'mossy') lithium on the anode surface... the absence of the signal at 262 ppm in the 7Li MAS NMR spectrum of the anode cycled at 45°C confirms the absence metallic lithium deposits

claims.claim: The main contribution to the mass loss of delithiated anodes below 185°C originates from adsorbed or embedded electrolyte components and their decomposition products, while beyond 185°C the decomposition of the binder dominates the mass loss.
claims.quote: the main contribution to the mass loss of the delithiated anodes below 185°C was determined to originate from adsorbed or embedded electrolyte components and their decomposition products. Beyond 185°C, the decomposition of the binder dominates the mass loss.

claims.claim: Cycling at higher current densities or higher cut-off potentials may reduce the thermal stability of cathodes and increase the reactivity during thermal runaway, but the moderate conditions applied in this study caused less aging effects on the cathode side.
claims.quote: constant-current cycling at higher current densities and cut-off potentials may reduce the thermal stability of cathodes and increase the reactivity during thermal runaway

claims.claim: When the heat dissipation coefficient of an NCM lithium-ion battery reaches 10 W/(m²·K), it can effectively prevent the occurrence of thermal runaway under local high-temperature heating conditions.
claims.quote: when the heat dissipation coefficient reaches 10 W/ (m2· K), the battery will not have thermal runaway. Therefore, improving heat dissipation capacity is an effective means to prevent thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Increasing the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery reduces the heat of reaction but does not prevent the occurrence of the pre-heat-runaway reactions (SEI decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction).
claims.quote: the increase of the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery will not prevent the occurrence of the pre-heat runaway reaction, but the heat of reaction is greatly reduced.

claims.claim: As the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery surface increases, the temperature distribution uniformity of the separator layer improves during the local heating process.
claims.quote: As the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery surface increases, the temperature distribution uniformity of the battery separator increases during the heating process. So, the uniformity of the separator in the test 4 is better than that in the test 3.

claims.claim: Under constant heating temperature, increasing the heating area increases the rate of material consumption and the reaction rate, but does not change the maximum temperature rise of thermal runaway or the type of side reactions occurring.
claims.quote: under the condition of constant heating temperature, the rate of reaction increases with the increase of heating area, and the rate of material consumption increases... As the heating temperature and heating area of the battery increased, the consumption rate of the material increased, but the maximum temperature rise and the type of side reaction did not change.

claims.claim: In all tested heating conditions (varying temperature, heat dissipation coefficient, and heating area), only SEI film decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction occurred as active side reactions; cathode decomposition, binder reaction, and electrolyte decomposition showed no contribution to heat generation.
claims.quote: the SEI film decomposition and the negative-electrolyte reaction are still the only two side reactions and the other three side reactions show no contribution to the heat generation.

claims.claim: Separator melting is identified as the significant symbol of thermal runaway because it causes internal short circuit, which leads to dramatic temperature increase.
claims.quote: the separator melting causes the internal short circuit which makes the temperature increase dramatically. As a result, the separator melting is the significant symbol of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The thermal model used in this study contains only five main thermal reactions, and the maximum temperature difference between experiment and simulation before the temperature jump is less than 10 K, with an error less than 2.5%.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature difference between experiment and simulation is less than 10K, and the error is less than 2.5%.

claims.claim: During heating TR of 16Ah NCM523, the soft package burst occurs at 96.4 °C, and the maximum temperature rise rate reaches approximately 65.03 °C/s at 392.8 s, with the battery reaching a maximum temperature of 420.2 °C.
claims.quote: In stage III, at 392 s, the TR of the battery occurs violently and the soft package burst at 96.4 ℃. At 392.8 s, the temperature rise rate is maximum, which is about 65.03 ℃/s in Fig. 2b. Thereafter, the temperature of 16Ah NCM523 increases rapidly, finally reaching the maximum temperature of 420.2 ℃ at 422.1 s.

claims.claim: During heating TR of 16Ah NCM523, the voltage remains stable for approximately 607.2 s before abruptly dropping to 0 V, providing no clear early warning and displaying a noticeable delay relative to heat production.
claims.quote: The voltage of 16Ah NCM523 remains stable for approximately 607.2 s before abruptly and rapidly dropping to 0 V. Consequently, it is evident that in the heating TR process, the voltage fails to provide a clear early warning, displaying a noticeable delay in response.

claims.claim: During overcharged TR of 16Ah NCM523, the voltage begins to drop rapidly 2 seconds earlier than the catastrophic critical temperature change rate, making voltage a faster early warning signal than temperature.
claims.quote: During overcharged TR, the voltage of 16Ah NCM523 begins to drop rapidly 2 s earlier than the catastrophe critical of temperature change rate.

claims.claim: During overcharged TR of 16Ah NCM523, after the temperature reaches 98.5 °C, it rapidly increases to a maximum of 481.4 °C within 27.4 s, with a maximum temperature rate of 65 °C/s.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rate of 16Ah NCM523 is recorded as 65 ℃/s at 12597 s and the temperature rapidly increases to a maximum of 481.4 ℃ within 27.4 s in stage III.

claims.claim: During nail penetration TR of 16Ah NCM523, the voltage drops from 4.1 V to 0.001 V within 15 s, temperature rises from 22.7 °C to 477.5 °C, and the maximum temperature rise rate reaches 159 °C/s at 110 s, with no apparent slow change process preceding these events.
claims.quote: During time from 101 to 116 s, the voltage of 16Ah NCM523 experiences a rapid decrease from 4.1 V to 0.001 V within 15 s in Fig. 6a... the temperature on the surface of 16Ah NCM523 starts to rise rapidly, starting from 22.7 ℃ at 106.9 s and reaching the highest temperature of 477.5 ℃ at 126.2 s. The increase rate of temperature also exhibits a rapid rise, going from 0 ℃/s at 107 s to maximal 159 ℃/s at 110 s... there is no apparent slow change process observed for both temperature and voltage of 16Ah NCM523 during nail penetration TR.

claims.claim: Among the three abuse conditions tested, nail penetration leads to the fastest TR, followed by heating, and overcharging leads to the slowest TR.
claims.quote: Among the abuse conditions, it is observed that nail penetration leads to the fastest TR, followed by heating, and finally overcharged.

claims.claim: Simulation results show that during heating TR, the SEI film begins decomposing at 94.1 °C, the positive active material begins reacting with the electrolyte at approximately 324 °C, and the electrolyte decomposes at approximately 360 °C.
claims.quote: In Fig. 9b, the SEI film starts decomposing at 94.1 ℃... As time goes by, the temperature reaches about 324 ℃, and the positive active material reacts with the electrolyte, releasing heat Qpos... When the temperature rises to about 360 ℃, the electrolyte decomposes and releases heat Qele.

claims.claim: During overcharged TR simulation of 16Ah NCM523, the heat inside the battery primarily originates from the reaction of the positive electrode material (Qpos), which is identified as the main cause of the temperature rise.
claims.quote: In Fig. 10c, it is observed that the heat inside the 16Ah 523NCM primarily originates from the reaction of the positive electrode material, represented by Qpos. This heat generation is the main cause of the temperature rise associated with overcharged TR.

claims.claim: A temperature of 77 °C and a temperature increase rate of approximately 1 °C/s are identified as the critical warning signals for overcharged TR of 16Ah NCM523.
claims.quote: The increasing rate of temperature at about 1 ℃/s and the temperature namely 77 ℃ can be the critical warning signal of overcharged TR of 16Ah NCM523.

claims.claim: For nail penetration TR, the shear effect of external forces causes multi-layer cracks and faults in the diaphragm, leading to internal short circuits.
claims.quote: the shear effect of external forces causes multi-layer cracks and faults in the diaphragm, leading to internal short circuits essentially

claims.claim: The safety ranking of lithium-ion batteries after external short circuit based on state of health is: 70% SOH is safest, followed by 100% SOH, then 80% SOH, with 90% SOH being the most hazardous.
claims.quote: The safety of batteries after ESC is ranked as: 70 % state of health (SOH) > 100 % SOH > 80 % SOH > 90 % SOH.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway after external short circuit occurs only to the battery with 90% SOH among the tested batteries (100%, 90%, 80%, 70% SOH).
claims.quote: Thermal runaway only occurs to battery with 90 % SOH after ESC.

claims.claim: After high-temperature cycling at 50°C, SEI growth causes the onset temperature of lithium-ion battery to increase, while Mn dissolution causes the thermal runaway temperature to decrease.
claims.quote: Onset temperature and thermal runaway temperature increases and decreases owing to solid electrolyte interphase growth and Mn dissolution.

claims.claim: The increase in resistance after high-temperature cycling aging affects heat generation during external short circuit less than other factors.
claims.quote: Increase of resistance after cycling affects heat generation during ESC less.

claims.claim: Fast discharging during ESC causes side reactions that generate heat in aged batteries, and this heat generation effect decreases with decreasing SOH below 90% SOH.
claims.quote: Fast discharging causes side reactions for aged batteries, which also generate heat. The heat generation effect of fast discharging decreases with the decrease of SOH after 90 % SOH.

claims.claim: Local micro internal short circuit causes more heat generation for aged batteries compared to fresh batteries.
claims.quote: Local micro internal short circuit causes more heat generation for aged batteries.

claims.claim: Mn is dissolved from the NCM cathode during high-temperature cycling and is transferred to the anode surface, as evidenced by the presence of Mn on the anode surface detected by EDS.
claims.quote: it is interesting to find that Mn occurs on the surface of anode. This means that the Mn is dissolved from the cathode and transferred to the anode.

claims.claim: New XRD peaks appear at 2θ = 38.88° and 44.8° in the cathode after high-temperature cycling, indicating crystal structure change caused by Mn dissolution.
claims.quote: new peaks occur at 2θ = 38.88◦ and 44.8◦. This means the crystal structure change of cathode. Mn dissolution causes the crystal structure change of cathode.

claims.claim: Battery shutdown during ESC at approximately 130°C surface temperature is attributed to shrinkage and melting of the separator, which causes micro internal short circuit, as separator shrink and melt occurs at approximately 125°C.
claims.quote: As the surface temperature of battery is about 130◦C, it is expected that the battery shutdown is caused by micro internal short circuit. It is reported that the shrink and melt of separator is about 125◦C. The shrink and melt of separator cause micro internal short circuit.

claims.claim: For the battery with 90% SOH after ESC, the surface temperature when discharging current starts to decrease to 0 A is 145.7°C, after which high temperature induces more exothermic side reactions and thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The temperature of battery with 90 % SOH is 145.7◦C when the discharging current starts to decrease to 0. Although the discharging current decreases to 0, high temperature induces more exothermic side reactions and thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Joule heat generation during ESC is ranked as 100% SOH > 80% SOH > 90% SOH ≈ 70% SOH, indicating that resistance increase in aged batteries affects Joule heat less and that discharging current is the primary determinant of Joule heat.
claims.quote: Fig. 7(e) shows that the Joule heat generation of batteries is ranked as: 100 % SOH > 80 % SOH > 90 %SOH ≈ 70 % SOH. This means that the resistance increase in Fig. 2(b-d) affects Joule heat of batteries less... the Joule heat generation is mainly determined by discharging current.

claims.claim: For the fresh battery and battery with 70% SOH, Joule heat from polarization resistance contributes the most to total heat generation during ESC, whereas for batteries with 90% and 80% SOH, heat generated by side reactions contributes the most.
claims.quote: Joule heat from polarization resistance contributes the most to the total heat generation for fresh battery. However, heat generated from side reactions contributes the most to total heat generation as thermal runaway occur... the heat from side reaction also increases more for battery with 80 % SOH. The contribution of different kinds of heat to the total heat generation for 70 % SOH is similar to fresh battery.

claims.claim: The hard external short circuit (resistance 0.562–0.788 mΩ) is the most hazardous form of ESC, and the severity of ESC decreases with increasing external conductor resistance.
claims.quote: the ESC was divided into three kinds: hard ESC (0.562, 0.788 mΩ), soft ESC (24 mΩ) and normal discharging (236, 821 mΩ). The disaster results of ESC decreased with the increase of resistance. This meant that the hard ESC was the most hazardous.

claims.claim: The thermal stability of lithium-ion batteries increases after cycling and storage at high temperature due to SEI growth, while fast charging, low-temperature cycling, and slight overcharging cycling decrease thermal stability due to lithium plating.
claims.quote: Thermal stability of lithium-ion battery increased after cycling and storage at high temperature owing to SEI growth. It was indicated that fast charging, low-temperature and slight overcharging cycling decreased thermal stability of lithium-ion battery owing to lithium plating.

claims.claim: The capacity of batteries with 100% SOH decreases suddenly near 90% SOH during high-temperature cycling at 50°C, attributed to serious degradation of cathode and anode.
claims.quote: The capacity decreases suddenly near 90 % SOH. This is caused by serious degradation of cathode and anode.

claims.claim: In LIBs, the SEI layer begins to chemically decompose above 80°C, while the separator undergoes chemical decomposition at temperatures exceeding 135°C.
claims.quote: A study by Chombo et al. [15] shows that the SEI in a LIB decomposes below 80°C, while the separator decomposes below 135°C.

claims.claim: Overdischarge occurs when the state of charge (SOC) drops below -10%, resulting in decomposition of the SEI that prevents direct contact between the anode and the electrolyte.
claims.quote: Overdischarge occurs when the state of charge (SOC) drops below −10 %, resulting in the decomposition of the SEI that prevents direct contact between the anode and the electrolyte [7].

claims.claim: When SOC exceeds 120%, SEI crystals grow large enough to physically damage the separator, triggering thermal runaway via a mechanism similar to mechanical damage.
claims.quote: When the SOC exceeds 120 %, the SEI crystals grow large enough to physically damage the separator. This situation mirrors mechanical damage, but the key difference is that during overcharging, the concentration near the electrode is already very high due to the SOC being 100 % or more.

claims.claim: A chemical reaction between lithium molecules in the anode and the electrolyte occurs at temperatures between 90°C and 120°C, producing oxygen and ethylene as products.
claims.quote: As shown in Eq. (25), a chemical reaction occurs between the lithium molecules in the anode and the electrolyte at temperatures between 90°C and 120°C, producing oxygen and ethylene [18].

claims.claim: Higher participation of the anode-electrolyte reaction (higher x value) decreases both adiabatic flame temperature and specific impulse during LIB thermal runaway, because more ethylene carbonate is consumed before combustion.
claims.quote: With a higher participation in the reaction, both the adiabatic flame temperature and specific impulse decrease. This is because an increase in the value of x signifies that more of the reaction in Eq. (25) has occurred, leading to a reduction in the amount of highly reactive ethylene carbonate.

claims.claim: Above a specific voltage threshold, total heat generation in an electrochemical system under isothermal conditions is linearly controlled by external voltage, due to Joule heating increasing linearly while electrochemical reaction heat remains constant.
claims.quote: The heat from the electrochemical reaction remains constant beyond the saturation point, while Joule heating increases linearly. Consequently, the total heat generation is linearly controlled by voltage.

claims.claim: An increase in potential difference results in an exponential reduction in IHP thickness due to increased electric field strength, with the rate of change becoming constant once voltage exceeds a threshold corresponding to the transport limit.
claims.quote: An increase in potential difference results in an exponential reduction in IHP thickness due to the increased electric field strength. The saturation point...represents the transport limit, which restricts the maximum rate of electrochemical reaction...where the voltage exceeds a certain threshold, the change in IHP thickness becomes constant.

claims.claim: The electrochemical reaction rate increases exponentially over a certain range as surface capacitance or surface charge increases, eventually plateauing, but this explicit relationship had not been previously reported.
claims.quote: Fig. 8 shows that the reaction rate increases exponentially at a certain range as surface capacitance or surface charge increases, eventually plateauing...the explicit relationship between electrochemical reaction rate and changes in surface capacitance or surface charge have not been reported earlier, thus making it a novel discovery.

claims.claim: The theoretical solution for IHP thickness and potential difference shows a potential difference of only 0.01 V compared to the linearized Poisson-Boltzmann cell model, corresponding to an error margin of 3.53%.
claims.quote: Compared to the theoretical solution presented in this study, the linearized Poisson-Boltzmann (PB) cell model—widely recognized for its high accuracy—shows a potential difference of only 0.01 V, corresponding to an error margin of 3.53 %.

claims.claim: When x values are below 0.7 during LIB thermal runaway combustion, increasing x increases the mole fraction of hydrogen and carbon monoxide while decreasing water vapor and carbon dioxide, posing secondary risks of carbon monoxide poisoning and hydrogen explosions.
claims.quote: When the x value is less than 0.7, increasing the x value leads to an increase in the mole fraction of hydrogen and carbon monoxide, while the quantities of water vapor and carbon dioxide decrease. This suggests that a higher participation in the reaction of Eq. (25) could lead to secondary risks, such as carbon monoxide poisoning and hydrogen explosions, in the event of a LIB fire.

claims.claim: The theoretical solution for IHP thickness cannot represent the detailed potential profile within the double layer; it can only calculate the IHP thickness and the potential difference between two points.
claims.quote: However, it cannot represent the detailed potential profile as one can only calculate the thickness of IHP and the potential difference between two points as shown in the figure.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition generally occurs at about 100°C, at which point the SEI starts to decompose and regenerate, constantly releasing heat and increasing the battery's internal temperature.
claims.quote: The first heat released is attributed to the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition, which generally occurs at about 100°C. At this moment, the SEI starts to decompose and regenerate, constantly releasing heat and increasing the battery's internal temperature.

claims.claim: The separator, normally manufactured with polypropylene or polyethylene, melts when the battery temperature achieves 120–130°C, causing a short circuit between anode and cathode.
claims.quote: the separator, normally manufactured with polypropylene or polyethylene, is melted when the battery temperature achieves 120–130°C. A short circuit occurs due to the inexistence of protection between the anode and cathode, producing more heat according to Joule's law.

claims.claim: NMC pristine batteries exhibited a maximum average temperature of approximately 753°C, an onset temperature of about 216°C, a maximum accumulated heat release of 28 kJ, and a maximum temperature rise rate of 3100°C/min during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature achieved on average was about 753°C, being the minimum onset and crucial temperatures about 216 and 247°C, respectively... the maximum accumulated heat released and temperature rise rate on average were 28 kJ and 3100°C/min, respectively.

claims.claim: Pristine LFP batteries did not exhibit sufficient self-sustaining exothermic reactions to induce thermal runaway during external heating tests, with maximum temperature remaining below 315°C.
claims.quote: LFP batteries did not exhibit a sufficient self-sustaining chemical reaction to induce thermal runaway during the heating tests... The maximum temperature was lower than 315°C, presenting lower values of temperature rise rate and accumulated heat release.

claims.claim: The greater thermal safety of LFP compared to NMC is attributed to the higher endothermic energy required to decompose the LFP cathode and to the lower oxygen released during cathode decomposition, which is about half the amount released by layered metal oxide cathodes such as NMC.
claims.quote: the oxygen released during cathode heating is less than layered metal oxide cathodes, accounting for half the amount of oxygen released when both are compared... The thermal safety of the LFP battery can be attributed to the endothermic reaction of the cathode decomposition and the less oxygen released during this process, resulting in less heat released when the oxygen reacts with the electrolyte.

claims.claim: SEI growth during aging consumes lithium and electrolyte in the anode, reducing the quantity of both components and thereby decreasing the heat released during the anode-electrolyte reaction (Reaction 5) in subsequent thermal runaway events.
claims.quote: The main outcome of this process is the reduction of the cycled lithium and the electrolyte present in the anode. The SEI growth increases the thickness using both elements, reducing the battery's capacity... the heat released is lower, 12.3% for the NMC and 68.9% for the LFP.

claims.claim: Batteries aged at 50°C showed greater capacity fading and greater reduction in heat release during thermal runaway compared to batteries aged at 20°C, with the heat release for batteries aged at 20°C being similar to that of pristine batteries.
claims.quote: the capacity fading was lower for both battery chemistries when compared to the result obtained at 50°C. The SEI growth is reduced, resulting in more mols of lithium and electrolyte in the anode... the values obtained were similar to the pristine batteries.

claims.claim: NMC aged batteries at 50°C ambient temperature showed a lower maximum temperature and temperature rise rate than pristine NMC batteries, but were triggered into thermal runaway at lower onset and crucial temperatures.
claims.quote: NMC aged batteries showed different results, especially for NMC aged battery at 50°C. The average maximum temperature and temperature rise rate achieved were lower than the pristine NMC batteries. In addition, the thermal runaway process was triggered before, as illustrated by the lower onset temperature and crucial temperature.

claims.claim: The aging model in this work considers only SEI growth as the mechanism for capacity fading, excluding lithium plating and material cracking, because those mechanisms are more relevant at low temperatures or very high current demands.
claims.quote: For the aging process, since only high temperature conditions (20°C and 50°C) are considered, the dominant mechanisms were simplified to only SEI growth. Other mechanisms like lithium plating are more relevant in conditions with problems for lithium allocation, like low temperature operation or very high current demands.

claims.claim: Low-temperature cycled battery (at -5°C) exhibits an average series resistance (Rs) growth of 73% compared to battery cycled at ambient temperature.
claims.quote: The low-temperature cycled battery exhibits significant growth of series resistance by an average of 73 %.

claims.claim: Low-temperature cycled battery (at -5°C) exhibits a charge transfer resistance (Rct) growth of 16% compared to battery cycled at ambient temperature.
claims.quote: growth in charge transfer resistance is 16 %

claims.claim: No significant change in SEI resistance (RSEI) was observed in the low-temperature cycled battery due to dead lithium formation, compared to the battery cycled at ambient temperature.
claims.quote: no significant change was observed in solid electrolyte interface (SEI) resistance due to the formation of dead lithium, compared to the battery cycled at ambient temperature.

claims.claim: At -5°C, the continuous rise in series resistance (Rs) is attributed to increased dead lithium formation, which can detach from the anode into the electrolyte and restrict ion flow.
claims.quote: The continuous rise in Rs at −5°C can be understood as more dead lithium forming at lower temperatures. It can get detached from the anode into the electrolyte, which restricts the flow of ions in the electrolyte.

claims.claim: The anode lithium deposit area fraction at -5°C (8.6%) is nearly twice that at 25°C (4.5%) after cycling, indicating significantly greater lithium deposition at lower charging temperatures.
claims.quote: The lithium deposit's area fraction, as depicted in Fig. 9b for cell tested at 25°C and Fig. 9c for cell tested at −5°C, were 4.5 % and 8.6 %, respectively. Notably, the latter value is nearly twice that of the lithium deposits observed at 25°C.

claims.claim: Transition metal (TM) content in the cathode is lower at 25°C (84.2%) than at -5°C (90.9%), indicating greater TM dissolution at higher temperature.
claims.quote: In the case of the cell tested at 25°C, the TM content is around 84.2 %, and for the cell at −5°C, 90.9 %. This shows that the highest dissolution of TMs occurred in the case of 25°C.

claims.claim: At low temperatures, slowed intercalation rate and reduced electrolyte conductivity promote deposition of lithium metal on the anode surface.
claims.quote: when the temperature drops, the intercalation rate (the rate at which lithium ions diffuse within the anode) and the electrolyte's conductivity within the battery both drop, which assists in the deposition of lithium metal on the anode surface.

claims.claim: At -5°C, the battery cell voltage almost immediately enters CV mode during charging due to very high resistance caused by slow solid-state lithium deposition, slow bulk electrolyte transport, and slow charge transfer kinetics.
claims.quote: The voltage of the cell tested at −5°C (Fig. 4c) almost enters the CV mode due to the very high resistance at a lower temperature because of the slow solid-state lithium deposition, slow bulk transport within the electrolyte and slow charge transfer kinetics.

claims.claim: Growth of metallic lithium dendrites can puncture the separator, which is a pathway to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The growth of metallic lithium can puncture the separator, which is responsible for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At 25°C, capacity increases up to approximately 41 cycles due to cracking of crystallites or breaking up of agglomerates allowing better electrolyte access to active material, after which capacity decreases.
claims.quote: at 25°C, the cell's capacity gradually increases till around 41 cycles, after which it starts to decrease. This is the same phenomenon observed in Ref. [43–45] due to the cracking of crystallites or the breaking up of agglomerates, allowing better access of the electrolyte to the active material.

claims.claim: At 60°C aging, accumulation of SEI decomposition products results in thicker SEI layers and shorter thermal runaway times compared to lower aging temperatures.
claims.quote: At 60°C, the accumulation of SEI decomposition products results in thicker SEI layers and shorter thermal runaway times.

claims.claim: At 80°C aging, SEI decomposition products are heavily transformed into particles with a loose structure, generating a large amount of gas that causes rupture of the aluminium-plastic film and evaporation of the electrolyte, resulting in longer duration of thermal runaway and lower maximum temperature.
claims.quote: At 80°C, the SEI decomposition products are heavily transformed into particles with a loose structure, generating a large amount of gas in the process, which further leads to the rupture of the aluminium-plastic film and the evaporation of the electrolyte, with a longer duration of thermal runaway and a lower maximum temperature.

claims.claim: The decomposition of metastable SEI layers, which occurs at 80–120°C, is regarded as the first side reaction during the thermal runaway process.
claims.quote: The decomposition of metastable SEI layers which occurs at 80–120°C is regarded as the first side reaction during the TR process.

claims.claim: Slow decomposition of SEI layers may have started below 60°C, with a possible onset temperature of 57°C, which is the first side reaction to occur when the temperature of the cell is increased.
claims.quote: It was considered that the slow decomposition of SEI layers may have started below 60°C, with a possible onset temperature of 57°C according to the literature, which is the first side reaction to occur when the temperature of the cell is increased.

claims.claim: The SEI layer is composed of stable components (LiF and Li2CO3) and metastable components (ROCO2Li and ROLi); the metastable component is transformed to the stable component at high temperatures (90–120°C) while releasing heat.
claims.quote: SEI layer is composed of stable component (LiF and Li2CO3) and metastable component (ROCO2Li and ROLi). The metastable component will be transformed to stable component at high temperatures (90–120°C) meanwhile release heat.

claims.claim: Cells aged at 80°C had SOH of 8% after 100 cycles, while cells aged at 25°C, 40°C, and 60°C had SOH of 93%, 90%, and 33% respectively after 100 cycles.
claims.quote: The SOH of the cells aged at 25°C, 40°C, 60°C and 80°C were 93%, 90%, 33% and 8% after 100 cycles, respectively.

claims.claim: Compared to the cell aged at 25°C, the heat release of cells aged at 40°C and 60°C decreased by 4% and 8% respectively, while that of the cell aged at 80°C decreased by 40%.
claims.quote: Compared to the cell aged at 25°C, the heat release of the cell aged at 40°C and 60°C decreased for 4% and 8% respectively, while that of the cell aged at 80°C decreased for 40%.

claims.claim: The activation energy of thermal runaway reactions for cells aged at 25°C, 40°C, 60°C, and 80°C were 0.853 eV, 0.851 eV, 0.887 eV, and 1.306 eV respectively, with a sharp increase only at 80°C aging.
claims.quote: The calculated activation energy of TR reactions for cells aged at 25°C, 40°C, 60°C, and 80°C are 0.853 eV, 0.851 eV, 0.887 eV and 1.306 eV, respectively. When the aging temperature is below 60°C, the activation energies of the TR reactions are almost equal... but it increased sharply when the aging temperature was 80°C.

claims.claim: For cells aged at 80°C, since most of the metastable SEI layer had been transformed into stable SEI layer during aging, self-heating was not detected until 105°C during ARC testing.
claims.quote: Since most of the metastable SEI layer has been transformed into stable SEI layer during the aging process, the self-heating was not detected until 105°C.

claims.claim: The amount of heat release during thermal runaway had less to do with the aging temperature itself, but more to do with whether the aluminium-plastic film broke during the aging process.
claims.quote: The amount of heat release had less to do with the aging temperature itself, but more to do with whether the aluminium-plastic film broke during the aging process.

claims.claim: The conclusions drawn in the paper apply only to the specific cells used in the experiment, and their validity for other cell types remains to be determined.
claims.quote: The conclusions drawn in this paper apply only to the cells used in this experiment. For other cell types, the validity of these conclusions remains to be determined.

claims.claim: Trace amounts of manganese (0.77% and 1.69% respectively) were detected on the separators of cells aged at 60°C and 80°C, indicating that dissolution of transition metals occurred on the cathode and migrated toward the anode.
claims.quote: trace amounts of manganese (0.77% and 1.69%, respectively) was also detected on the separator aged at 60°C and 80°C, which indicated that the dissolution of transition metals occurred on the cathode, then entered the electrolyte over cycling and migrated to the vicinity of the anode.

claims.claim: The physics-based model reduces the mean absolute error (MAE) of capacity prediction by 33.6% compared to a semi-empirical model over 500 cycles for an NMC cell.
claims.quote: The semi-empirical model exhibited an MAE of 0.00982 Ah over the first 500 cycles, whereas our physics-based model's MAE was only 0.00652 Ah, representing a 33.6 % reduction in prediction error.

claims.claim: SEI concentration is highest at the interface with the separator and lowest at the current collector side of the negative electrode after 500 cycles at 1C rate.
claims.quote: the SEI concentration is highest at the interface with the separator and lowest at the current collector side of the negative electrode.

claims.claim: Higher C-rates increase the SEI reaction current density and exacerbate its non-uniform distribution on the anode, with the effect being significantly more pronounced near the separator than near the current collector.
claims.quote: the SEI generation near the current collector shows little variation across different C-rates, but the difference is notably larger in the anode region close to the separator. In other words, the C-rate significantly influences the SEI concentration distribution, with higher C-rates leading to more pronounced non-uniformity.

claims.claim: After 500 charge-discharge cycles at 1C, the EC concentration decreases by 10%, and the exchange current density reduces with cycle number, causing the SEI reaction current density to also decrease progressively.
claims.quote: after 500 charge-discharge cycles, the EC concentration has decreased by 10 %. Correspondingly, Fig. 5(b) illustrates that the exchange current density reduces with the number of cycles. According to Eq. (14), as the exchange current density decreases, the reaction current density also decreases.

claims.claim: The model underestimates capacity fade for the NMC cell after 500 cycles because degradation mechanisms not included in the model, such as lithium plating and loss of active material, become more pronounced during later cycling stages.
claims.quote: A slight discrepancy is observed for the NMC cell after 500 cycles, where the model underestimates capacity fade compared to experimental data. This deviation is likely due to the combined effects of degradation mechanisms not included in the current model, which become more pronounced during later stages of cycling. One such mechanism is lithium plating... Another contributing factor is active material isolation.

claims.claim: Under abusive conditions, SEI decomposition can trigger exothermic reactions that may lead to thermal runaway, posing serious safety risks.
claims.quote: Under abusive conditions, SEI decomposition can trigger exothermic reactions that may lead to thermal runaway, posing serious safety risks.

claims.claim: Porosity near the separator of the negative electrode becomes blocked earlier than regions near the current collector after extended cycling, due to more pronounced SEI formation near the separator, ultimately leading to battery failure.
claims.quote: the porosity near the separator is expected to become blocked earlier than in regions near the current collector after extended cycling, ultimately leading to battery failure. In support of this, Yang et al. [26] investigated the evolution of anode porosity due to the deposition of side reaction products. They found that the porosity near the separator became blocked after 3000 cycles.

claims.claim: The standard carbonate electrolyte (1.0 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC) is ignited immediately upon contact with a fire source and burns until the electrolyte is consumed, whereas the DT-pF electrolyte (0.5 M LiDFOB + 0.5 M LiTFSI in FEC/ETFEC, 1:1 by vol) is completely incombustible and cannot be ignited during flammability testing.
claims.quote: For the traditional commercial carbonate electrolyte (SC, 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC, 1:1 by vol), it is ignited immediately upon contact with the fire source, and the fire is not extinguished until the electrolyte burns out. In contrast, the designed weakly solvated dual salt electrolyte (DT-pF, 0.5 M LiDFOB + 0.5 M LiTFSI in FEC/ETFEC, 1:1 by vol) is completely incombustible, which cannot be ignited during the entire flammability test

claims.claim: In the standard carbonate electrolyte (SC), the Li+ solvation shell is dominated by solvent molecules with a Li-OEC coordination number of 2.89, and the salt anion (PF6−) is excluded from the solvation shell, exhibiting a solvent-separated ion pair (SSIP) structure.
claims.quote: It can be observed that the coordination of Li-OEC dominates in the standard electrolyte with the largest coordination number of 2.89, while the salt anion (PF6−) is pushed out of the solvated shell, showing the characteristics of solvent separation ion pair (SSIP) structure.

claims.claim: In the DT-pF electrolyte, the total coordination number of anions around Li+ is 2.41, which significantly exceeds the coordination number of FEC solvent molecules (1.43), indicating an anion-dominated Li+ solvation shell.
claims.quote: The total coordination number of anions in DT-pF electrolyte system is 2.41, which is significantly higher than that of FEC solvent molecule (1.43), indicating that the structure of Li+ solvated shell is dominated by anions in DT-pF electrolyte, with some fluorinated solvent molecules involved.

claims.claim: The LUMO energy level of LiDFOB (−3.260 eV) is substantially lower than those of LiPF6 (−1.500 eV) and LiTFSI (−1.539 eV), indicating that LiDFOB has a lower energy barrier for reductive decomposition on SiOx anode surfaces.
claims.quote: The LUMO energy levels of LiPF6, LiTFSI and LiDFOB are −1.500 eV, −1.539 eV and −3.260 eV, respectively, indicating that the energy barriers of reduction decomposition of LiTFSI and LiDFOB on the surface of SiOx anodes are relatively low.

claims.claim: In the SC electrolyte, LixPFy formed from LiPF6 decomposition is unstable and degrades into PF5, a strong Lewis acid that catalyzes decomposition of carbonate molecules to produce HF (via PF5 + ROH(H2O) → HF + RF + OPF3), causing serious corrosion to SiOx anodes.
claims.quote: LixPFy is unstable and is easy to be decomposed into LixPOFy and PF5. The latter is a strong Lewis acid, which will catalyze the decomposition of carbonate molecules to produce hydrogen fluoride (PF5 + ROH (H2O) → HF + RF + OPF3), and cause serious corrosion to SiOx

claims.claim: After 300 cycles at 1.0 A/g, the SiOx anode in DT-pF electrolyte retains 1073.7 mAh/g with a capacity retention rate of 65.2%, whereas in SC electrolyte it retains only 224.8 mAh/g with a retention rate of 17%.
claims.quote: the cell in SC electrolyte experiences a rapid capacity degradation process within 200 cycles, and only 224.8 mAh g−1 is remained after 300 cycles, with a low capacity retention rate of 17%... the DT-pF electrolyte enables a capacity of 1073.7 mAh g−1 after 300 cycles with the capacity retention rate up to 65.2%.

claims.claim: After 500 cycles, the SiOx electrode in SC electrolyte exhibits an electrode thickness expansion rate of approximately 320%, while the SiOx electrode in DT-pF electrolyte shows a much lower expansion rate of approximately 40%.
claims.quote: the significantly increased electrolyte thickness (with an expansion rate of ≈320%)... the expansion problem of the electrode is also effectively alleviated, with a low expansion rate of ≈40%.

claims.claim: LiBO2 and Li2B4O7, formed by decomposition of oxalate borate in the SEI, have higher calculated Young's moduli (LiBO2: 168.24 GPa; Li2B4O7: 248.08 GPa) and lower Li+ migration activation energies (LiBO2: 0.973 eV; Li2B4O7: 0.942 eV) than LiF (117.06 GPa; 1.091 eV).
claims.quote: LiBO2 and Li2B4O7 have higher calculated Young's modulus (LiF: 117.06 GPa, LiBO2: 168.24 GPa, and Li2B4O7: 248.08 GPa) and lower lithium ion migration activation energy than LiF (LiF: 1.091 eV, LiBO2: 0.973 eV, Li2B4O7: 0.942 eV), which is conducive to further promoting rapid migration of Li+ in the SEI film

claims.claim: High concentration of LiTFSI in the T-pF electrolyte causes corrosion of the ternary NCM811 cathode, leading to severe capacity degradation in SiOx||NCM811 full cells.
claims.quote: The severe capacity degradation of T-pF electrolyte loaded cell can be attributed to the corrosion of ternary cathode by high concentration of LiTFSI

claims.claim: The EIS of cycled SiOx||LFP full cells shows no SEI-related semicircle in the high-frequency region for the SC electrolyte, indicating that the SC electrolyte fails to form an effective SEI film on SiOx anodes.
claims.quote: a new semicircle related to the SEI interface resistance (RSEI) appears in the high frequency region of the Nyquist diagram of cycled SiOx||LFP full cells using the designed perfluorinated electrolytes, while there is no semicircle observed in the Nyquist diagram of the cycled cells using the standard electrolyte (SC). This difference indicates that the SC electrolyte failed to form the effective SEI film on the surface of SiOx anodes

claims.claim: SEI decomposition initiates around 80-120°C and has a peak near 100°C, making it one of the first exothermic reactions during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: SEI decomposition is one of the first exothermic reactions to occur during thermal runaway, initiating around 80-120°C and having a peak near 100°C.

claims.claim: Anode decomposition occurs at temperatures exceeding 250°C, generating more heat than SEI-related reactions and constituting a significant portion of total heat during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Anode decomposition occurs at temperatures exceeding 250°C, leading to the collapse of the graphite structure and the reaction between intercalated lithium and electrolyte. This reaction generates more heat than previous reactions involving SEI and constitutes a significant portion of the heat generated during battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Rapid heating occurs in the anode around 280°C due to the reaction between the anode and electrolyte.
claims.quote: Yamaki et al. confirmed through experiments comparing various samples that rapid heating occurs in the anode around 280°C due to the reaction between the anode and electrolyte.

claims.claim: The thermal stability ranking of cathode materials from highest to lowest is: LFP > LMO > NMC111 > NCA > LCO.
claims.quote: according to Doughty et al., the thermal stability comparison among these materials ranks as follows: LFP > LMO > NMC111 > NCA > LCO, indicating higher thermal stability in this order.

claims.claim: Increasing the nickel content in NMC cathode materials decreases their thermal stability.
claims.quote: the increase in nickel content in NMC, which is pursued for higher energy density, is deteriorating the thermal stability of NMC.

claims.claim: PE/PP-based separators begin to melt in the range of approximately 130-190°C, and separator melting is an endothermic reaction with enthalpy of -90 J/g for PE and -190 J/g for PP.
claims.quote: PE/PP-based separators begin to melt when they reach their melting points, which generally range from approximately 130-190°C. The melting of separators is an endothermic reaction, and the enthalpy of the reaction has been reported as -90 J/g when PE melts and -190 J/g when PP melts.

claims.claim: Ceramic coatings on separators can raise the collapse temperature to approximately 200-260°C compared to the 130-190°C range of uncoated PE/PP separators.
claims.quote: The melting point of separators enhanced with ceramic coatings can be increased to around 200-260 degrees Celsius.

claims.claim: Under thermal abuse conditions above 170°C, phase transformations occur throughout the NMC cathode, leading to the release of lattice oxygen.
claims.quote: under thermal abuse conditions above 170°C, the phase transformations occur throughout the cathode, leading to the release of lattice oxygen.

claims.claim: In the 78Ah NMC/Graphite pouch cell simulation, cumulative heat from anode decomposition was 448.7 kJ, cathode decomposition 356.6 kJ, internal short circuit 51.2 kJ, and SEI decomposition 13.6 kJ.
claims.quote: The heat generated from the anode decomposition was the highest at 448.7 kJ, followed by 356.6 kJ from the cathode decomposition, 51.2 kJ from the short circuit, and 13.6 kJ from the SEI decomposition.

claims.claim: In the parametric study of cathode materials, NMC111 produced the highest maximum temperature (945.7°C) and total heat generation (794.3 kJ), while LFP produced the lowest maximum temperature (568.2°C) and total heat generation (446.8 kJ).
claims.quote: The maximum temperature and heat generation were highest for NMC, followed by LMO, LCO, and LFP, suggesting that LFP is relatively safe and NMC is vulnerable cathode material in terms of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: In the module CFD simulation, insulation pads延长 the time intervals between thermal runaway onset in adjacent cells: the intervals between cells 3→4, 6→7, and 9→10 (separated by insulation pads) were 74.35 s, 75.60 s, and 72.25 s, respectively, compared to 8.70–32.05 s for non-padded adjacent cell pairs.
claims.quote: The time intervals for thermal runaway between cells 3 and 4, cells 6 and 7, and cells 9 and 10 are longer than those between other cells, indicating that the insulation pads are effectively delaying the propagation of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: In the module CFD simulation, vent gas convection increased the average air temperature inside the module from 25°C to approximately 500°C during initial thermal runaway and exceeded 800°C after all cells underwent thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Initially, the average temperature inside the module was 25°C, but as thermal runaway began, it rose to approximately 500°C and exceeded 800°C after all cells underwent thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The simulation model omits combustion of vent gas, which is identified as a limitation that may underestimate heat generation during thermal runaway propagation within the module.
claims.quote: since combustion was not considered in this simulation, it was simulated as described. If combustion were considered, it could be expected that the interaction of the high-temperature conditions inside the module and the flammable, explosive vent gas would generate additional heat.

claims.claim: Higher degrees of lithiation of graphite, larger amounts of electrolyte, and higher salt concentrations all lead to increased heat generation during anode decomposition.
claims.quote: They found that higher degrees of lithiation of the graphite, larger amounts of electrolyte, and higher concentrations of salt lead to increased heat generation.

claims.claim: The LFP decomposition onset temperature for carbon-coated LiFePO4 is 245°C with a reaction enthalpy of 250 J/g.
claims.quote: Zaghib et al. reported that the onset temperature for thermal decomposition of carbon coated LiFePO4 is 245°C, with a reaction enthalpy of 250 J/g.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway occurs for fully charged NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC at 249°C, but not for fully charged AC//HC Li-HSC under the same ARC test conditions.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway occurs for fully charged NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC at 249 ◦C, but not for fully charged AC//HC Li-HSC.

claims.claim: The O2 released by the NCM component accelerates self-heating reactions and is identified as the rooted thermal runaway trigger of the NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC.
claims.quote: The O2 releasing by NCM component accelerates the self-heating reactions, and the introduction of NCM into cathode is the rooted thermal runaway trigger of NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC.

claims.claim: The NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC reaches a peak thermal runaway temperature (Tpeak) of 490.9°C with smoke spurting out and does not catch fire or explode during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC shows the highest runaway temperature (Tpeak) of 490.9 ◦C with smoke spurting out and does not catch fire or explode during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At the peak thermal runaway temperature (Tpeak), the ratio of hydrocarbon gases and O2 decreases while that of CO and CO2 increases, due to oxidation reactions with carbonate solvent.
claims.quote: When the temperature rose to the Tmax, the ratio of hydrocarbon gases and O2 decreased, while that of CO and CO2 increased (Fig. 5d). The main reason is the oxidation reactions with the carbonate solvent, releasing CO, CO2.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate for the AC//HC Li-HSC during ARC test is 5.5°C min⁻¹, which does not meet the thermal runaway standard of 1°C s⁻¹, indicating thermal runaway did not occur in the AC//HC Li-HSC.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature rise rate of the whole process is 5.5 ◦C min−1, which does not meet the standard of thermal runaway of 1 ◦C s−1, indicating that thermal runaway did not occur in the AC//HC Li-HSC.

claims.claim: The PET/ceramic nonwoven separator can withstand thermolysis up to 350°C, and demonstrates more thermal stability than polyethylene and polypropylene separators.
claims.quote: The TG curve also demonstrates that the PET/ceramic separator can withstand thermolysis up to 350 ◦C (Fig. 2b). The DSC and TGA results confirmed that the PET/ceramic separator demonstrates more thermal stability than polyethylene and polypropylene separators.

claims.claim: The separator collapses at about 190°C, causing an internal short circuit that instantaneously releases electrical energy and generates a great deal of heat, leading to rapid temperature increase.
claims.quote: The separator collapses at about 190 ◦C, internal short circuit releases the electric energy of the cell instantaneously and generates a great deal of heat, leading to the rapid temperature increase.

claims.claim: The O2/N2 ratio in the gas collected from NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC during thermal runaway is much higher than that of air, resulting from the decomposition of NCM cathode and SEI film.
claims.quote: The ratio of O2/N2 is much higher than that of air, resulting from the decomposition of NCM cathode and SEI film.

claims.claim: With the presence of electrolyte, the exothermic reaction of delithiated NCM/AC cathode starts at an onset temperature of 150°C, with maximum exothermic peak at approximately 293°C.
claims.quote: However, with the presence of the electrolyte, the exothermic reaction of delithiated NCM/AC cathode starts earlier with the onset temperature of 150 ◦C (Fig. 7a). The first and the maximum exothermic peaks are observed at approximately 207 and 293 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: The aluminum plastic film of the NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC pouch cell ruptured at 129.6°C, causing a drop in the temperature rising rate due to release of produced gases including vaporized electrolyte solvent.
claims.quote: the cell swelled gradually, and then aluminum plastic film ruptured at 129.6 ◦C (Fig. 5b, left), whereby vessel pressure increased slightly. The release of produced gas including vaporized electrolyte solvent leads to the reduction of the reaction rate, and the temperature rising rate slows down.

claims.claim: Lithium plating occurs at the anode surface when the anode potential exceeds the threshold value of 0 V vs. Li/Li+.
claims.quote: Lithium plating is described as the precipitation of the lithium metal, which occurs at the surface of the anode once the anode potential exceeds the threshold value of 0 V (vs. Li/Li+)

claims.claim: Formation of lithium dendrites during lithium plating can cause tearing of the separator, leading to short circuit and instant battery failure.
claims.quote: the formation of lithium dendrites during lithium plating can bring about the tearing of the separator, subsequent short circuit, and instant battery failure.

claims.claim: SEI layer growth is expedited at lower anode potentials and elevated temperatures.
claims.quote: The SEI layer growth can generally be expedited at the lower anode potentials and the elevated temperatures.
