claims.claim: Manganese dissolution from Mn-based cathodes deposits onto the anode surface and intensifies electrolyte decomposition, leading to charge loss in lithiated carbon-based anodes.
claims.quote: the dissolved manganese ions will migrate to the anode and will be deposited onto the electrode surface and/or compounded in the SEI layer. This intensifies the process of electrolyte decomposition leading to the charge loss in lithiated carbon-based anode

claims.claim: Manganese dissolution from spinel-structured manganese cathode is of greatest significance at elevated temperatures and charged states.
claims.quote: it is of the greatest significance to the spinel structured manganese cathode, specifically at elevated temperatures and charged states

claims.claim: Separator pore clogging caused by electrolyte decomposition products accounts for approximately 10% of total cell impedance rise.
claims.quote: the products of electrolyte decomposition, forming a film on the separator and thereby clogging the pores and impeding Li-ion transport, are partially responsible for the cell resistance increase and stand for almost 10% of the total cell impedance rise.

claims.claim: Separator pore closure occurs at temperatures in the range of 130 to 150°C, stopping the charge/discharge process by hindering ion transport between electrodes.
claims.quote: High temperatures (often the range of 130 to 150°C) cause the softening of separator's films and the closing of their pores, resulting in the stoppage of charge/discharge process through hindering the ion transport between the electrodes.

claims.claim: For a trilayer PP/PE/PP separator, impedance increase begins at approximately 130°C (PE melting point) and continues until approximately 165°C (PP melting point).
claims.quote: The impedance increase took place at around the melting point of PE (i.e., 130°C) and proceeded until the melting point corresponding to PP (i.e., 165°C).

claims.claim: Separator accounts for approximately 15% of the overall cell impedance increase during aging or cycling at elevated temperatures.
claims.quote: separator stands for around 15% of the overall cell impedance increase. They later revealed that decline in separator ionic conductivity originated from the clogging of separator pores caused by the electrolyte decomposition products (SEI layer formation), which was expedited at elevated temperatures

claims.claim: Copper current collector begins to oxidize and dissolve at cell voltages of around 1 V, with dissolved copper ions plating onto the anode during subsequent recharging.
claims.quote: The potential of copper current collector at extremely low voltages of the cell (around 1 V) rises to the point at which the copper will initiate to be oxidized and dissolved in the form of copper ions within electrolyte. Then, dissolved copper ions during subsequent recharging plate as copper metal onto the anode surface

claims.claim: EC:DMC electrolyte caused a greater density of pitting corrosion on aluminum current collectors than PC:DEC electrolyte, achieving higher attack density in only 150 cycles compared to over 690 cycles for PC:DEC.
claims.quote: cycling in EC:DMC electrolyte led to an even greater density, only by 150 cycles... EC:DMC electrolyte appeared to be more corrosive (lower value of pitting resistance, Rpit) as compared to the other electrolyte composed of PC:DEC solvent during several hundred cycles

claims.claim: A 10°C increase in temperature can double the rate of cell degradation, according to the Arrhenius relationship.
claims.quote: a 10°C escalation in temperature can double the rate of cell degradation.

claims.claim: Cell cycle life begins to decline gradually when operating below approximately 10°C or above approximately 60°C, and thermal runaway is threatened at nearly 70°C.
claims.quote: cycle life begins to decline gradually as a result of cell operation at temperatures lower than around 10°C, and upper than about 60°C; however, the cell would encounter the thermal runaway threat at nearly 70°C.

claims.claim: LiPF6 salt decomposes in the presence of moisture to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), which causes decay of the electrolyte and electrodes.
claims.quote: As a result of the exposure to moisture, LiPF6 salt will be decomposed into hydrofluoric acid (HF), which exhibits a very high reactivity causing to decay the electrolyte and electrodes alike.

claims.claim: Volume change in graphite caused by lithium insertion and extraction is expected to be less than 10%, depending on the material.
claims.quote: The volume changes in graphite, caused by the insertion and extraction of lithium ions is not expected to be significant (less than 10%, depending on the material)

claims.claim: Both gravimetric and volumetric energy densities of Li-ion batteries are nearly two times those of Ni-MH batteries.
claims.quote: both gravimetric and volumetric energy densities for Li-ion batteries are nearly two times the Ni-MH batteries.

claims.claim: Copper susceptibility to environmentally assisted cracking (EAC) can be eliminated by appropriate control of metallurgical conditions, specifically excluding combinations of large grain size and work-hardening.
claims.quote: the exclusion of each of these conditions could eliminate any susceptibility. Accordingly, it is perceptible that the copper susceptibility to EAC may be removed through appropriate attention to the metallurgy.

claims.claim: In-situ TEM experiments require a vacuum environment that prevents the use of liquid electrolyte, so solid electrolytes are typically used instead.
claims.quote: in-situ TEM and SEM experiments require vacuum environment which renders using a liquid electrolyte impossible during these experiments. Therefore, in-situ experiments under vacuum, usually solid electrolytes are used

claims.claim: In LiFePO4/graphite batteries overcharged at 1C rate, the number of pores in the separator is significantly reduced when the SOC reaches 120%, resulting in increased internal resistance.
claims.quote: The number of pores in the separator is significantly reduced when the SOC reaches 120 %, resulting in increased internal resistance.

claims.claim: Joule heat is the main heat source for most of the time before internal short circuit (ISC) during overcharge of LiFePO4/graphite batteries, and plays a key role in the occurrence of ISC.
claims.quote: Joule heat was the main heat source for most of the time before ISC, which contributed a key role to the occurrence of ISC.

claims.claim: In a 100% SOH LiFePO4/graphite battery overcharged at 1C rate, the ISC occurs at approximately 130% SOC and thermal runaway occurs at approximately 135% SOC, meaning ISC and TR do not occur simultaneously.
claims.quote: When the battery was overcharged to about 130 % SOC, an ISC occurred in the battery... When the battery was overcharged to about 135 % SOC, it was determined based on the temperature profile that TR had occurred.

claims.claim: The ISC in LiFePO4/graphite batteries starts from the positive collector side because the impedance of the positive aluminum foil is higher than that of the negative copper foil, causing local high temperature that melts the separator.
claims.quote: The area on the positive collector side was damaged most seriously at 130 % SOC (after ISC), so the ISC may start from the positive collector side. This was because the impedance of the positive aluminum foil was higher than that of the negative copper foil, and the local high temperature caused the separator to melt.

claims.claim: The heat release from SEI decomposition in LiFePO4/graphite batteries increases from 11.5 J·g⁻¹ at 100% SOH to 97.6 J·g⁻¹ at 55% SOH after ISC, due to SEI film thickening during battery aging.
claims.quote: the heat generation of SEI decomposition (peak 2#) increased gradually from 11.5 J⋅g−1 of 100 % SOH to 97.6 J⋅g−1 of 55 % SOH with the decrease of SOH. This was because the SEI film thickened during battery aging due to continuous rupture and repair of SEI.

claims.claim: The heat release from the reaction between lithiated graphite and binder (QLi-bin) decreases with decreasing SOH from 75% to 55% (259.0 J·g⁻¹ to 51.2 J·g⁻¹) due to the decrease in lithium content in graphite.
claims.quote: the corresponding heat release decreased from 259.0 J⋅g−1 of 75 % SOH to 51.2 J⋅g−1 of 55 % SOH due to the decrease of lithium content in graphite.

claims.claim: The heat release from the reaction between lithiated graphite and binder in a 100% SOH LiFePO4/graphite battery increases from 102.6 J·g⁻¹ at 100% SOC to 228.5 J·g⁻¹ at 120% SOC, then decreases to 182.0 J·g⁻¹ at 130% SOC due to lithium reduction caused by ISC.
claims.quote: With the increase of SOC from 100 % to 120 %, the heat release of peak 4# increased significantly from 102.6 J⋅g−1 to 228.5 J⋅g−1. However, when the SOC increased from 120 % to 130 %, the exothermic heat for the peak 4# decreased to 182.0 J⋅g−1 due to the decrease of the lithium content in the graphite caused by the ISC.

claims.claim: Among LiFePO4/graphite batteries with SOH ranging from 100% to 60%, the TR onset temperature is lowest at 70% SOH when overcharged at 1C rate, indicating 70% SOH batteries may be most prone to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the battery was overcharged at 1C rate, the initial temperature of battery TR first decreased and then increased with the decrease of SOH, and it was the lowest at 70 % SOH.

claims.claim: When approaching ISC during overcharge of LiFePO4/graphite batteries, the heat generation power of side reactions exceeds that of internal resistance, after which ISC occurs.
claims.quote: When the battery was overcharged to about 1092 s, the heat generation power of the side reaction was greater than that of the internal resistance, and then the ISC occurred at 1119 s.

claims.claim: Cathode active material shedding in LiFePO4/graphite batteries during overcharge is attributed to oxidative decomposition of PVDF binder, which begins when the electrode potential reaches approximately 5 V at 120% SOC.
claims.quote: The shedding of the cathode material may be attributed to the oxidative decomposition of PVDF binder... when the potential reached 5 V, PVDF would gradually decompose, and the higher the potential, the faster the decomposition rate.

claims.claim: After ISC in LiFePO4/graphite batteries, QSEI (SEI decomposition heat) + QLi-ele (lithium and electrolyte reaction heat) together with QLi-bin (lithiated graphite and binder reaction heat) determine the difference in thermal runaway behavior across batteries of different SOH.
claims.quote: After ISC, QSEI (SEI decomposition heat) + QLi−ele (lithium and electrolyte reaction heat) and QLi−bin (lithiated graphite and binder reaction heat) together determine the difference in thermal runaway (TR) behavior of different SOH batteries.

claims.claim: For LiFePO4/graphite batteries at 130% SOC, both cathode and anode weight loss reached up to 14% during TG analysis, indicating large gas production during overcharge.
claims.quote: the weight loss of the anode increased significantly by 14.0 % when the SOC increased to 130 %... The weight loss of 130 % SOC cathode reached 13.9 % at 300 ℃.

claims.claim: At a low C-rate (0.5 C), ohmic heat, reversible heat, and reaction heat account for 47%, 30.7%, and 22.3% of total heat generation respectively at SOC = 40%, but reversible heat dominates total heat generation after SOC = 50%, reaching 71.5% at full charge.
claims.quote: At the head of state of charge (SOC = 40 %), ohmic heat, reversible heat, and reaction heat account for 47 %, 30.7 %, and 22.3 % of total heat generation, respectively. However, the reversible heat continues to rise after SOC = 40 % and dominates the total heat generation after SOC = 50 %. Its proportion is up to 71.5% at the fully charged.

claims.claim: At a high C-rate (2.0 C), ohmic heat is the dominant factor contributing to heat generation throughout charging, because current density is the only variable in the ohmic heat equation.
claims.quote: It can be seen that the ohmic heat is the dominant factor in contributing heat generation. Although the reversible heat also rises markedly after SOC = 40%, the ohmic heat still generates more heat than the reversible heat. According to Eq. (19), the current density is the only variable found in this equation. It is thus recognized that the current density is more influential for heat generation at high C-rate.

claims.claim: The heat generation of the cathode is greater than that of the anode at full charge (SOC = 100%), which is caused by the low solid phase conductivity of the cathode (0.1 S cm-1), which is 10 times less than that of the anode.
claims.quote: the solid phase conductivity of the cathode is 0.1 S cm-1, which is 10 times less than the anode. So, the heat generation of the cathode is greater than that of the anode, which is caused by the low solid phase conductivity for the cathode.

claims.claim: SEI film thickness increases with increasing C-rate, and a higher C-rate causes faster SEI film growth due to higher current density for the side reaction.
claims.quote: it can be clearly observed that the thickness of SEI film increases with raising the current density... Increasing the C-rate will lead to the growth rate of the SEI film faster.

claims.claim: The SEI film heat generation value is too small to affect the temperature of the lithium-ion battery during cycling, regardless of C-rate.
claims.quote: it is observed the value of heat generation in Fig. 13 is too small to affect the temperature of the lithium-ion battery during cycling.

claims.claim: SEI film thickness increases by approximately 70 nm over 500 cycles with increasing C-rate cycling, based on C-C and C-O bond lengths of 154 pm and 143 pm respectively.
claims.quote: The thickness of SEI is increased by around 70 nm using the C-C and C-O bond lengths as 154 pm and 143 pm, respectively.

claims.claim: Paraffin PCM (PPCM) delays the onset of thermal runaway by 277 seconds compared to a cell without PPCM, by absorbing heat during phase change.
claims.quote: PPCM delays the onset of TR by 277 s and lowers the battery temperature utilizing its heat absorption

claims.claim: The presence of flammable PPCM increases the peak heat release rate from 8.52 kW (cell without PPCM) to 29 kW, a factor of approximately 3.4 times.
claims.quote: the main HRR peak of cell with PPCM reached 29 kW, which was 3.4 times higher than that without PPCM (8.52 kW)

claims.claim: Adding AMTH (aluminium trihydrate and magnesium hydroxide hybrid, 50 wt%) to PPCM reduces peak heat release rate from 29 kW to 15.5 kW, a 47% reduction, while maintaining TR onset delay close to that of pure PPCM.
claims.quote: the cell with PPCM-AMTH showed a peak HRR of 15.5 kW, which is 47% lower than that with PPCM

claims.claim: CPCM, which has higher thermal conductivity (5.023 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹) than PPCM (0.151 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹), promotes rather than delays thermal runaway onset, reducing TR onset time to 385 s versus 401 s for the bare cell.
claims.quote: For the cell covered with CPCM, the TR onset time was 385 s, which is shorter than that of the cell without CPCM. It suggests that covering with CPCM promotes rather than delaying the TR occurrence.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC NCA 18650 cells under 40 kW·m⁻² external heating, peak cell surface temperature at thermal runaway deflagration reached 904 °C, while 30% SOC and 0% SOC cells reached lower peaks of 790 °C and 679 °C respectively.
claims.quote: the highest cell surface temperatures at TR deflagration increase with the increasing SOC

claims.claim: Adding APP (ammonium polyphosphate, 50 wt%) to PPCM reduced peak HRR by only 8% (from 29 kW to 23.9 kW) and accelerated TR onset to 267 s, the earliest of all test conditions.
claims.quote: the peak HRR was 23.9 kW for the cell with PPCM-APP, just an 8% decrease comparing to that of PPCM (29 kW)...the cell with PPCM-APP was the first to go into the TR at 267 s compared to other conditions

claims.claim: The ignition point of paraffin is around 160 °C, while battery temperatures during thermal runaway and fire can exceed 500 °C, creating conditions where PCM is ignited during TR events.
claims.quote: The ignition point of paraffin is around 160 ℃ while the temperature of batteries that fail into TR and fire could go beyond 500 ℃

claims.claim: PPCM has a latent heat of 196 kJ·kg⁻¹ and thermal conductivity of 0.151 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, while CPCM has a lower latent heat of 135 kJ·kg⁻¹ and much higher thermal conductivity of 5.023 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹.
claims.quote: For CPCM in Fig. 7(a), the latent heat is 135 kJ∙kg−1, which is lower than that of PPCM (196 kJ∙kg−1)...the thermal conductivity of CPCM is 5.023 W∙m−1∙K−1, which is much higher than that of PPCM (0.151 W∙m−1∙K−1)

claims.claim: The amount of PCM used in the current study is relatively large, which limits its practical applicability in real battery thermal management systems.
claims.quote: One thing that should be noted is that the amount of PCM is relatively large in current study, which is difficult to apply in the practical production and application for BTMS.

claims.claim: When PPCM melts and is ejected during violent TR deflagration, burning molten PPCM can fall onto surrounding areas and potentially trigger fire propagation if combustible materials are present nearby.
claims.quote: accompanied by the violent TR deflagration and fast jet fire, a lot of burning melt PPCM was ejected out of the container. The ejected PPCM fell onto surrounding places of the test rig, which expanded the burning range. In this case, if there were combustible materials around, it would be potential for PPCM to trigger fire propagation

claims.claim: SEI decomposition begins at about 350 K, and the positive electrode material reacts with the electrolyte at about 500 K, as found through accelerated calorimeter experiments.
claims.quote: Jia et al. [9] find that the SEI decomposition begins at about 350 K, and the positive electrode material reacts with the electrolyte at about 500 K.

claims.claim: The reaction between graphite and electrolyte begins at approximately 393 K with a high specific enthalpy of 1.7 MJ/kg.
claims.quote: Park et al. [10] use differential scanning calorimetry to study the reactions between the graphite and electrolyte, and find that the reaction begins at approximately 393 K, with a high specific enthalpy of 1.7 MJ/kg.

claims.claim: The decomposition temperature of the LiPF6-electrolyte system is 523 K.
claims.quote: Zhang et al. [11] conclude that the decomposition temperature of the LiPF6-electrolyte system is 523 K.

claims.claim: The NE-E reaction exhibits three exothermic peaks (P1, P2, P3), where P2 is induced by the PE-E reaction and P3 is induced by electrolyte decomposition.
claims.quote: the NE-E reaction heat release can be seen. Although the LiC6 in the negative electrode precedes the reaction between the positive electrode active material and the electrolyte, the HRR of the NE-E reaction QNE−E begins to decline after reaching the first exothermic peak (P1 in Fig. 6(b)). It only takes 34 s for the PE-E reaction to have the HRR from 50 J/s to the peak. At this time, QNE−E increases to the second peak (P2 in Fig. 6(b)) because of the temperature rise caused by the heat liberation from the reaction between LiCoO2 and electrolyte... Subsequently, QNE−E declines. The electrolyte decomposition also reaches the peak rapidly at 1908 s, which leads to the third HRR peak (P3) from the NE-E reaction.

claims.claim: The PE-E reaction contributes more heat as fuse energy to trigger thermal runaway, while NE-E reaction and electrolyte decomposition mainly account for the runaway energy.
claims.quote: PE-E reaction contributes more heat for fuse energy to trigger TR, but the NE-E reaction and electrolyte decomposition mainly accounts for the runaway energy.

claims.claim: No thermal runaway is observed when the equivalent heat transfer coefficient h is greater than 12.5 W/(m² K) in the simulations at oven temperature 433.15 K.
claims.quote: No TR is observed when h is greater than 12.5 W/(m2 K) in our simulations.

claims.claim: Increasing SEI and electrolyte decomposition volume fraction intensity has no effect on the TR maximum temperature.
claims.quote: increasing SEI and electrolyte decomposition intensity is found no effect on the TR temperature.

claims.claim: Reducing the volume fraction of negative electrode active material (fNE) by 5.0% will significantly reduce thermal runaway risk in terms of temperature and time.
claims.quote: Reducing the fNE−E within 5.0 % will significantly reduce TR risk.

claims.claim: The SEI decomposition completes under all heat transfer coefficient conditions tested (h = 2.5–12.5 W/(m² K)), releasing the same total heat of 835.9 J in every case.
claims.quote: the SEI is decomposed completely under all h conditions... If we integrate the QSEI over the time, the heat under all h conditions is the same, i.e., 835.9 J.

claims.claim: With a 7.5% reduction in negative electrode active material volume fraction (NE-L1 case), the maximum battery temperature reaches only 494 K, compared to 561 K in the baseline case, indicating significantly diminished TR risk.
claims.quote: Tmax reaches 494 K at 2495 s in the NE-L1 case... Consequently, the risk of LIB TR is significantly diminished.

claims.claim: Reducing the positive electrode active material volume fraction fPE by 7.5% (PE-L1 case) delays time to maximum temperature by 183 s and reduces maximum temperature by 74 K compared to the baseline, but this delay is shorter than that achieved by an equivalent reduction in fNE.
claims.quote: there is a delay of 183 s in the tT−max and 74 K of reduction on the tT−max in the PE-L1 case. Comparing with the counterpart of NE-L1 and NE-L2 cases in Fig. 10, it can be concluded that with a same reduction on the fPE and fNE... reducing the volume fraction of the negative electrode material more effectively delays the battery from reaching Tmax, providing more time for thermal management.

claims.claim: The electrolyte decomposition occurs at the end of the thermal runaway transient due to its relatively large activation energy of 2.74×10⁵ J/mol and high decomposition temperature of 527 K.
claims.quote: The decomposition of electrolyte occurs at the end in a TR transient because of the relatively large activation energy (2.74e5 J/mol) with the high electrolyte decomposition temperature (527 K).

claims.claim: At h = 2.5 W/(m² K), the NE-E reaction contributes 57.1% of total heat, and at h = 5.5 W/(m² K) it contributes 50.6%, making it the largest heat contribution among the four side reactions in both TR cases.
claims.quote: In the two TR cases (2.5 and 5.5 W/m2•K), the NE-E reaction makes the largest contributions for temperature increase, which is 57.1 % at h = 2.5 (W/m2•K) and 50.6 % at h = 5.5 (W/m2•K).

claims.claim: Even at h = 20 W/(m² K) without thermal runaway, the NE-E reaction accounts for 73.1% of total heat among all side reactions, remaining the dominant heat contributor.
claims.quote: Even if h increases to 20 W/(m2 K), this ratio (73.1 %) is still the largest among all side reactions.

claims.claim: The LiC6 specific enthalpy of 1.714×10⁶ J/kg is the largest among all four side reactions considered in the model.
claims.quote: the largest specific enthalpy of the reaction (1.714e6 J/kg) among all reactions.

claims.claim: At 60°C, formation of thicker solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layers was observed in lithium-ion batteries, along with an increased risk of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: At 60°C, the formation of thicker solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layers was observed, along with an increased risk of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Temperatures reaching 80°C caused SEI decomposition and transformation into loosely structured particles, prolonging thermal runaway and lowering peak temperatures.
claims.quote: Temperatures reaching 80°C caused SEI decomposition and transformation into loosely structured particles, prolonging thermal runaway and lowering peak temperatures.

claims.claim: Lithium plating lowers the self-heating initiation temperature and accelerates thermal runaway by weakening the cathode due to the dissolution of transition metals.
claims.quote: Lithium plating, a common issue at higher temperatures, lowers the self-heating initiation temperature and accelerates thermal runaway by weakening the cathode due to the dissolution of transition metals.

claims.claim: Low-temperature cycling with varying discharge rates showed more significant capacity degradation at lower discharge rates, challenging the assumption that lower temperatures always lead to reduced aging.
claims.quote: low-temperature cycling with varying discharge rates showed more significant capacity degradation at lower discharge rates, challenging the assumption that lower temperatures always lead to reduced aging.

claims.claim: Capacity loss over 210 days at 55°C was directly related to state of charge (SOC), with higher SOCs causing greater degradation, and post-mortem analysis revealed thickened SEI layers and increased resistance in batteries stored at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The research showed that capacity loss over 210 days at 55°C was directly related to state of charge (SOC), with higher SOCs causing greater degradation. Post-mortem analysis revealed thickened SEI layers and increased resistance in batteries stored at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: Aged cells from high-temperature cycling experienced thermal runaway later than fresh cells, while low-temperature aged cells showed worse thermal runaway behaviors.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway tests showed that aged cells from high-temperature cycling experienced thermal runaway later than fresh cells, while low-temperature aged cells showed worse thermal runaway behaviors.

claims.claim: Current estimation methods are deemed insufficient for capturing complex aging interactions, specifically the way temperature, charge rates, and usage combine to induce aging.
claims.quote: the current estimation methods are deemed insufficient for capturing complex aging interactions. The way that many elements, such as temperature, charge rates, and usage, combine to induce aging is not always captured by current approaches.

claims.claim: Characterization measurements performed using small currents (below 1C) temporarily improved battery performance by increasing capacity and decreasing resistance, attributed to improved cell kinetics such as faster ion transport and reduced resistance.
claims.quote: characterization measurements, performed using small currents (below 1C), temporarily improved battery performance by increasing capacity and decreasing resistance. This effect was attributed to improved cell kinetics, such as faster ion transport and reduced resistance.

claims.claim: In LiFePO4 batteries at -10°C, higher charging currents and cut-off voltages accelerate capacity degradation primarily due to lithium deposition.
claims.quote: Insights from cycle life tests on LiFePO4 batteries at -10°C confirm that higher charging currents and cut-off voltages accelerate capacity degradation, primarily due to lithium deposition.

claims.claim: Silicon's reactivity accelerates calendar aging in lithium-ion cells, and little is known about time-dependent degradation (calendar aging) in silicon-based batteries.
claims.quote: This Perspective highlights studies showing that silicon's reactivity accelerates calendar aging in lithium-ion cells... While much research has focused on mitigating volume changes in silicon anodes during cycling, little is known about time-dependent degradation (calendar aging).

claims.claim: In SC-NCM811|Gr cells, the perfluorinated electrolyte increased the thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 by 12.5 °C compared to the conventional electrolyte (from 198.7 °C to 211.2 °C).
claims.quote: for the battery using a perfluorinated electrolyte, T2 increased by 12.5 °C and T3 decreased by 41.2 °C.

claims.claim: In SC-NCM811|Gr cells, the perfluorinated electrolyte decreased the maximum thermal runaway temperature T3 by 41.2 °C compared to the conventional electrolyte (from 692.8 °C to 651.6 °C).
claims.quote: T3 decreased by 41.2 °C... After a few seconds, the battery reached the highest temperature T3 (692.8 °C).

claims.claim: The combination of SC-NCM811 cathode with conventional electrolyte produced a DSC exothermic peak at 224.7 °C with an enthalpy change of 172.2 J·g−1, approximately twice that of the perfluorinated electrolyte combination (83.85 J·g−1).
claims.quote: The combination of the cathode and conventional electrolyte produced a sharp exothermic peak at 224.7 °C, with an enthalpy change (ΔH) of 172.2 J·g−1... the reaction enthalpy was half that of the conventional electrolyte, 83.85 J·g−1.

claims.claim: No CO2 emission was observed during the exothermic process for the cathode combined with perfluorinated electrolyte, confirming the absence of a redox reaction between the perfluorinated electrolyte and cathode.
claims.quote: CO2 emission was not observed in the cathode and perfluorinated electrolyte during the exothermic process, confirming that there was no redox reaction between the perfluorinated electrolyte and cathode.

claims.claim: The conventional electrolyte advanced the oxygen production temperature of the SC-NCM811 cathode by approximately 50 °C and released a larger amount of oxygen gas compared to the pure cathode, indicating acceleration of the cathodic phase transition.
claims.quote: The use of a conventional electrolyte would advance the oxygen production temperature by ∼50 °C and release a large amount of oxygen gas, indicating that the conventional electrolyte would accelerate the rate of the cathodic reaction and phase transition.

claims.claim: After 200 cycles, the CEI layer on the SC-NCM811 cathode cycled in the perfluorinated electrolyte was 8 nm thick and uniform, whereas the CEI layer in the conventional electrolyte was 20 nm thick and uneven with visible impurities.
claims.quote: a dense, uniform, and clean CEI layer (8 nm) was formed on the surface of the cathode circulating in the perfluorinated electrolyte... the CEI layer formed on the surface of the cathode circulating in the conventional electrolyte is 20 nm thick and uneven with visible impurities.

claims.claim: In Sony 18650 cells, a 'knee' in the ARC heating rate around 110°C is associated with breakdown of the anode SEI layer followed by exothermic reduction of the electrolyte by the lithiated carbon.
claims.quote: A 'knee' in the heating rate around 110 ◦C has been associated with breakdown of the anode solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer followed by exothermic reduction of the electrolyte by the lithiated carbon.

claims.claim: Gen1 cells using MCMB graphite anode showed greater thermal stability than commercial Sony cells in ARC testing, with cells at 50% SOC or lower showing no accelerated heating.
claims.quote: The ARC data, vertically offset in Fig. 3, show that the Gen1 cells have greater thermal stability than seen for the commercial Sony cells. The cells with 50% or lower SOC showed no accelerated heating, only transient heating spikes.

claims.claim: Gen2 cells with flaky MAG10 graphite anode showed continuous accelerated heating starting below 50°C due to an ineffective SEI layer, similar to Sony cell performance.
claims.quote: The ﬂaky Gen2 MAG10 carbon particles developed an ineffective SEI layer that allowed initiation of continuous thermal runaway as low as 50 ◦C, very similar to the performance of the Sony cells.

claims.claim: Al-stabilized Gen2 cathode (LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2) showed reaction peaks approximately 25°C higher than Gen1 cathode (LiNi0.85Co0.15O2) for both charged and discharged states.
claims.quote: The Gen2 cathodes, with the Al stabilized LiNi0.80Co0.15 Al0.05O2, showed reaction peaks about 25 ◦C higher than seen for the Gen1 cathodes for both charged and discharged materials.

claims.claim: Aging of Gen2 anodes reduced the DSC heat of reaction from 2222 J/g (unaged) to 857 J/g after 8 weeks at 45°C/80% SOC, while further aging to 20 weeks at 55°C/80% SOC yielded only a comparable 903 J/g, indicating rapid initial reduction followed by slow further change.
claims.quote: Integration of these exotherms showed that the heat of reaction decreased from 2222 J/g for the unaged anode to 857 and 903 J/g for the 8- and 20-week aged cells, respectively. The effects of aging occur quickly at the anodes but then progress at a comparably slow rate.

claims.claim: Gen2 cathode material showed little reduction in exothermic reaction enthalpy after 8 weeks aging (1287 J/g vs. 1350 J/g unaged), with more notable change only after 20 weeks (1228 J/g), indicating cathode aging requires more extended time than anode aging.
claims.quote: Initial aging during the 8 week period had little effect on the magnitude of the exotherm and the temperature of the reaction. The cathode of the cell aged at 55 ◦C for 20 weeks showed a similar reaction enthalpy, 1228 J/g, compared to 1287 J/g for the 8-week aged cathode.

claims.claim: For Gen2 cells, the anode contributes 53% and the cathode 41% of the total cell reaction enthalpy, while free electrolyte contributes a maximum of 6%.
claims.quote: The anode material is shown to have the highest contribution to the overall cell reaction but the cathode is of comparable magnitude. The free electrolyte contributes a maximum of 6% to the total reaction enthalpy.

claims.claim: In the 100% SOC cell, internal pressure increases starting at approximately 70°C due to electrolyte evaporation, before safety venting occurs at 1.79 MPa.
claims.quote: as the temperature further increases (reaching approximately 70 °C), the internal pressure also starts to increase as a consequence of electrolyte evaporation. Then, the SEI layer begins to decompose... Safety venting occurs as more and more gases are generated inside the cell, causing a rapid increase of the internal pressure to 1.79 MPa until the pressure exceeds the threshold of the safety valve.

claims.claim: In the 0% SOC cell, thermal runaway (Stage III) does not occur and maximum internal temperature reaches only 330°C, due to insufficient stored energy.
claims.quote: Unlike the thermal runaway stages for the 100% and 50% SOC cases, thermal runaway (Stage III) does not occur in the cell and the maximum internal temperature reaches only to 330 °C. Interestingly, there is no (at least not clear) second pressure peak, due to lack of stored internal energy.

claims.claim: The maximum internal-to-surface temperature difference during thermal runaway exceeds 180°C in the 100% SOC cell because transient heat generation cannot rapidly dissipate.
claims.quote: The maximum internal-to-surface temperature difference (ΔTmax) increases to over 180 °C, because the transient generation of heat that cannot rapidly dissipate.

claims.claim: The turning section of the differential curves of internal temperature and pressure, occurring at approximately 70–80°C for all SOCs, identifies the transition from reversible to irreversible reactions and defines a safety warning range before safety venting.
claims.quote: the 'turning section' characterizes the enhanced electrolyte evaporation and the early onset of irreversible SEI decomposition at temperature around 70 ~ 80 °C (detailed data in Table S2) for all cases of SOCs. A safety warning range is thereby set to begin from electrolyte evaporation and to end with SEI decomposition, a range where irreversible reactions have not yet occurred.

claims.claim: The FBG is insensitive to pressure below 2 MPa (sensitivity of −5.6 pm MPa⁻¹), while the FPI is insensitive to temperature with a sensitivity of only 0.5 pm °C⁻¹ over 25–600°C, enabling decoupled simultaneous temperature and pressure measurement.
claims.quote: FBGs in silica fibers are insensitive to pressure, especially for pressures below 2 MPa... For a large temperature change from 25 to 600 °C, the wavelength shift of the FPI is less than 0.3 nm, corresponding to a negligible temperature sensitivity of 0.5 pm °C−1.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition has an onset temperature of 80°C, and the SEI decomposition temperature is lowered as the state of charge (SOC) decreases.
claims.quote: The SEI decomposition has an onset temperature of 80 °C but some reports suggest it may even begin at a lower temperature. The SEI decomposition temperature is lowered as the state of charge (SOC) decreases.

claims.claim: At 100–120°C, the electrolyte will decompose and/or the separator will begin to melt, and during a resulting short circuit, as much as 70% of the cell energy can be released in less than 1 minute.
claims.quote: eventually at 100–120 °C, the electrolyte will decompose and/or the separator will begin to melt... During a short circuit, as much as 70% of the cell energy can be released in less than 1 min, causing the temperature to increase further.

claims.claim: Above 150°C, cathode decomposition can become self-sustaining if heat cannot be dissipated quickly enough, placing the cell fully in thermal runaway with exponentially increasing temperature.
claims.quote: Above 150 °C, this reaction can become self-sustaining if the heat cannot be dissipated away quickly enough, and the cell is now fully in TR (Figure 2) with an exponentially increasing temperature.

claims.claim: The cell temperature can approach 900°C during the final stage of thermal runaway when all cell materials including the anode and cell casing decompose.
claims.quote: As the temperature increases further, eventually, all cell materials, including the anode and cell casing, will decompose. The cell temperature can approach 900 °C during this step.

claims.claim: LiFePO4 cathode is stable up to 310°C and produces the lowest volume of gas during thermal runaway compared to other cathode materials such as layered lithiated metal oxides.
claims.quote: The most stable cathode material in current use is LiFePO4 (LFP), which is stable up to 310 °C... LFP also produces the lowest volume of gas during TR.

claims.claim: For LCO–graphite cells with 33, 1000, and 3300 mAh capacities, the energy released during thermal runaway is in proportion to the capacity of the cell, and the 33 mAh cell underwent incomplete thermal runaway.
claims.quote: An accelerated calorimetry study of LCO–graphite cells with 33, 1000, and 3300 mAh capacities showed that the energy released during TR is in proportion to the capacity of the cell; the 33 mAh cell underwent incomplete TR.

claims.claim: Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) separator undergoes a reversible thermal expansion above 80°C, causing a tenfold increase in resistance over a 10°C temperature increase, and does not melt until 322°C.
claims.quote: Research on improved thermal shutdown separators has recently focused on polyacrylonitrile (PAN), which does not melt until 322 °C, but undergoes a reversible thermal expansion above 80 °C, causing a tenfold increase in resistance over a 10 °C temperature increase.

claims.claim: Extreme events such as cell rupture and short circuit may cause a release of energy beyond which the cooling system can mitigate, making cooling systems insufficient as a sole safeguard.
claims.quote: It is also important to note that extreme events such as cell rupture and short circuit may cause a release of energy beyond which the cooling system can mitigate.

claims.claim: Aged cells exhibit lower TR triggering temperatures and higher heat generation than fresh cells under thermal abuse when compared at equivalent normalized usable capacity (UE).
claims.quote: aged cells exhibit lower TR triggering temperatures and higher heat generation than fresh cells under thermal abuse with elevated thermal risks

claims.claim: Under fast-charging conditions (3 C), severe lithium plating on graphite anodes accelerates TR initiation by promoting early reactions of lithium plating with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: under fast-charging conditions (e.g., 3 C), severe lithium plating on graphite anodes has been shown to accelerate TR initiation by promoting the early reactions of lithium plating with the electrolyte

claims.claim: Batteries aged at elevated temperatures (45 °C) show delayed TR triggering due to stabilization of the SEI, whereas lower-temperature aging (20 °C) is associated with significant lithium plating leading to earlier TR initiation.
claims.quote: batteries aged at elevated temperatures (45 °C) show delayed TR triggering due to the stabilization of the SEI, whereas lower-temperature aging (20 °C) is associated with significant lithium plating, leading to earlier TR initiation

claims.claim: Lithium plating occurs when anode potential is lower than 0 V vs. Li/Li+, and a decrease in cycling temperature lowers the threshold for lithium plating to occur.
claims.quote: High charging/discharging rates and low temperatures may be favorable to lithium plating thermodynamically when the anode potential is lower than 0 V vs. Li/Li+... A decrease in cycling temperature lowers the threshold for lithium plating to occur.

claims.claim: For batteries aged at 25 °C, TR triggering temperature decreases from 254.6±2.8 °C at 0.2 C to 246.9±3.0 °C at 2 C, showing a clear negative correlation between TR triggering temperature and increasing cycling C-rate.
claims.quote: For batteries aged at ambient temperature (25 °C), Ttr decreases from 254.6±2.8 °C under the 0.2 C aging condition to 246.9±3.0 °C at 2 C, showing a clear negative correlation between TR triggering temperature and increasing cycling rate.

claims.claim: For batteries cycled at elevated temperature (45 °C), TR triggering temperature initially increases from 256.0±4.2 °C at 0.2 C to 261.1±4.9 °C at 0.5 C before declining with further C-rate increases, exhibiting non-monotonic behavior.
claims.quote: for batteries cycled at elevated temperature (45 °C), Ttr initially increases from 256.0±4.2 °C (0.2 C condition) to 261.1±4.9 °C (0.5 C condition), before declining with further increases in C-rate.

claims.claim: The exothermic reaction between plated lithium and electrolyte predominantly occurs near 100 °C, which is substantially lower than the typical TR triggering temperature (~250 °C), meaning lithium plating alone cannot fully account for enhanced heat generation observed after TR initiation.
claims.quote: the exothermic reaction between plated lithium and electrolyte predominantly occurs near 100 °C, which is substantially lower than the typical TR triggering temperature (~250 °C). To reconcile this discrepancy, additional sources of energy release during the post-triggering phase must be considered.

claims.claim: At UE = 80%, the fraction of post-triggering heat generation (gTR) is much higher in aged cells (70.1–74.4%) compared to fresh cells (56.4%), indicating heightened thermal hazards in aged cells at lower usable energy.
claims.quote: gTR is much higher in aged cells (i.e., 74.4%, 73.6%, and 70.1%) compared to fresh cells (i.e., 56.4%) at UE = 80%, indicating heightened thermal hazards in aged cells at lower UE.

claims.claim: PE-based separators melt at temperatures above 130°C, leading to internal short circuit in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: the melting of PE-based separators when temperature increases to above 130 °C, which also leads to internal short circuit

claims.claim: Electrolyte decomposition occurs at temperatures above 200°C and voltages of approximately 4.6 V due to carbonate organic solvents with low flash points and low boiling points, generating large amounts of heat.
claims.quote: the electrolyte is easily decomposed at high temperature (> 200 °C) and high voltage (about 4.6 V) due to the carbonate organic solvents with low flash points and low boiling points, which will generate large amount of heat

claims.claim: For LixCoO2, if the lithium content x is less than 0.16, the material reacts violently with electrolytes, releasing large amounts of gases and heat.
claims.quote: Zeng et al. reported that LixCoO2 could be deeply charged to oxidative CoO2, but x = 0.16 is a critical point. If the value x is less than 0.16, LixCoO2 will react with electrolytes violently, releasing a lot of gases and heat.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of phase transition for NMC811 starts at approximately 135°C, compared to approximately 260°C for NMC111.
claims.quote: the onset temperature of NMC111 is about 260 °C, but the first phase transition of NMC 811 starts at about 135 °C

claims.claim: When the anode potential reaches the oxidation potential of copper around 3.5 V (vs. Li+/Li) during deep overdischarge, the copper current collector is oxidized and Cu2+ ions dissolve into the electrolyte, migrate across the separator, and can deposit as Cu dendrites on the cathode, potentially causing internal short circuit.
claims.quote: once the anode potential reaches the oxidation potential of copper around 3.5 V (vs. Li+/Li), the copper current collector will be oxidized and Cu2+ ions will be dissolved into the electrolyte and migrate across the separator. Then the Cu2+ ions will be reduced during the subsequent charging process, leading to the deposition of Cu dendrites on the cathode. With the growth of Cu dendrites, the separator will be penetrated, resulting in self-short and even triggering a thermal runaway

claims.claim: SEI decomposition begins at approximately 60°C, and at 90°C the SEI completely decomposes with intensified side reactions generating large amounts of gases and heat.
claims.quote: When the temperature is above 60 °C, the SEI starts to decompose and the exposed lithiated anode materials (LiC6 as an example) will rapidly react with the electrolyte... As the temperature goes up to 90 °C, the SEI will completely decompose and side reactions will intensify, generating a lot of gases and heat.

claims.claim: The volumetric change of NCM811 secondary particles during cycling (7.8% ± 1.5%) is larger than that of NCM111 (3.3% ± 2.4%), attributed to a decrease in the interlayer lattice parameter c from 14.467 to 14.030 Å.
claims.quote: Volume change of NCM811 secondary particles (7.8% ± 1.5%) is even larger than that of NCM111 (3.3% ± 2.4%), which could be attributed to obvious decrease in the interlayer lattice parameter c from 14.467 to 14.030 Å.

claims.claim: The presence of electrolyte reduces the onset decomposition temperature of delithiated Li1-x(Ni1/3Mn1/3Co1/3)0.9O2 from 278°C to 197°C.
claims.quote: Compared to pure NMC, the mixed one in the presence of electrolytes decomposed much earlier with the onset temperature reducing from 278 to 197 °C.

claims.claim: A gas sensor detected LIB cell failure earlier than a temperature sensor during an overcharging experiment when the cell temperature was below 60°C.
claims.quote: during the overcharging experiment, the gas sensor detected the failure earlier than the temperature sensor when the cell temperature was below 60 °C

claims.claim: Lithium plating on the anode surface produces an extra volume increase of 0.37 cm3 Ah-1, while intercalating 1 Ah of lithium into graphite causes a volume change of only 0.12 cm3.
claims.quote: Lithium plating will bring about a gain in volume of 0.37 cm3 Ah−1, while charging 1 Ah by intercalating lithium will cause a volume change of 0.12 cm3.

claims.claim: Lithiated silicon (LixSi) is essentially safer than lithiated graphite (LixC6), attributed to the formation of a thermally inert LiF layer generated through electrolyte consumption.
claims.quote: it is revealed that lithiated silicon (LixSi) is essentially safer than lithiated graphite (LixC6). This enhanced stability is attributed to the formation of a thermally inert LiF layer, generated through electrolyte consumption

claims.claim: At 100% SOC for LSO-0, the dominant Stage III anode-electrolyte exotherm onset occurs at 152.8°C, and mild thermal runaway (dT/dt > 1°C/min) occurs at 320.3°C.
claims.quote: The dominant Stage III occurs at 152.8°C, and the cell temperature keeps rising until the final thermal failure. If we define a mild thermal runaway when dT/dt is greater than 1°C/min, given the small capacity of the four-coin-cell stack, this event occurs at 320.3°C

claims.claim: For LSO-20 at 100% SOC, the anode-binder reaction peak heat generation rate is 0.023 W/g at ~160°C, which is lower than the LSO-0 value of 0.031 W/g at ~165°C, indicating Si suppresses binder decomposition heat release.
claims.quote: For LSO-20, the binder decomposition peak shifts to ~160°C, with a magnitude of 0.023 W/g, indicating the influence of Si on altering the anode-binder interaction. The peak at LSO-30 occurs earlier (~155°C) and is less intense (0.006 W/g)

claims.claim: Silicon oxide in the Si-C anode reacts with HF (a thermal decomposition product of LiPF6 electrolyte) to form thermally stable SiF4 on the anode surface, which may cover the binder and mitigate its thermal decomposition.
claims.quote: silicon oxide can react with hydrogen fluoride (HF), a typical product from the thermal decomposition of the LiPF6 electrolyte, to form silicon fluoride (SiF4) on the surface of composite graphite anode. Given the high bonding energy of the Si-F chemical bond, this SiF4 inert layer is thermally stable. It may cover the anode binder to mitigate its thermal decomposition.

claims.claim: In large-format 1 Ah pouch cell simulations, moderate Si doping (10–20% LSO) lowers the thermal runaway onset temperature T2 due to enhanced anode-silicon reactions, while higher Si content (~30%) raises T2 by suppressing SEI decomposition and anode-binder exotherms.
claims.quote: moderate Si doping (10–20%) lowers the thermal runaway onset temperature due to enhanced anode-silicon reactions. Conversely, higher Si content (~30%) raises the onset temperature by suppressing other exothermic reactions.

claims.claim: Excessive Si content (30% LSO) shortens the time interval between initial self-heating onset and thermal runaway in the medium SOC range (50–80%) while extending it at other SOC levels.
claims.quote: excessive Si content (30%) shortening the interval between initial self-heating exotherm and thermal runaway in the medium SOC range (50 to 80%) while extending it at other SOC levels.

claims.claim: At 30% SOC for LSO-30, Stage I (SEI decomposition) is present between 137.3°C and 145.1°C, whereas Stage I disappears for LSO-20 at the same 30% SOC, indicating greater thermal instability with higher LSO content at low SOC.
claims.quote: At 30% SOC, Stage I occurs between 137.3°C and 145.1°C. Given that Stage I disappears in the LSO-20 case at the same 30% SOC, this observation further substantiates that the Si-C anode with LSO-30 suffers a more severe thermal instability.

claims.claim: In a fully enclosed two-battery confined system, thermal runaway propagated within 203.7 s, compared with 369.9 s in the battery-smoke decoupled case, representing a 44.9% reduction in propagation time.
claims.quote: Experimentally, thermal runaway propagated within 203.7 s in a fully enclosed system, compared with 369.9 s in the decoupled case.

claims.claim: In the confined two-battery system (Test 4#), direct conductive heat transfer between batteries accounted for 80.5% of total energy input triggering thermal runaway in the secondary cell, while smoke-mediated convection and radiation contributed 7.6% and 12%, respectively.
claims.quote: The contributions from Qc, Qh, and Qr were 34,741 J, 3260 J, and 5162 J, respectively, accounting for 80.5 %, 7.6 %, and 12 % of the total heat triggering Bat2's TR.

claims.claim: Smoke retention in a confined system caused a 366.4°C increase (103% elevation) in ambient temperature compared to the smoke-decoupled configuration.
claims.quote: In Test 2#, Te surged to 722.4 ℃ after TR, exceeding the corresponding value in Test 1# by 366.4 ℃, representing a 103 % increase.

claims.claim: Although smoke-mediated heat transfer (convection and radiation) constituted only 19.5% of the heat input to the secondary battery, the elevated ambient temperature from smoke retention inhibited heat dissipation from the secondary battery, promoting heat accumulation and accelerating thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: Although Qsmoke numerically constitutes only 19.5 % of the heat input triggering TR in Bat2 in Test 4#, the elevated ambient temperature Te inhibits potential heat dissipation from Bat2, promoting heat accumulation and accelerating TRP.

claims.claim: Total energy released during thermal runaway of the 62 Ah NMC cell was 911,400 J, of which 48.0% (434,659 J) was carried away by ejected gases and particulates, and 52.0% (474,106 J) remained as residual heat in the cell.
claims.quote: In total, the heat carried away by the eruptive gases and particulates, Qv, sums to 434,659 J, approximately 48.0 % of the total energy QTR released during TR. The residual heat retained within the cell, Qs is 474,106 J, constituting the remaining 52.0 %.

claims.claim: In the battery-smoke decoupled test (Test 3#), conductive heat from the trigger battery accounted for 96.9% of the total heat absorbed by the secondary battery prior to thermal runaway, with convective and radiative contributions from smoke at only 1.5% and 1.7%, respectively.
claims.quote: Conductive heat Qc from Bat1 accounted for 82,072 J, representing 96.9 % of the total heat input. Due to the smoke from Bat1 being exhausted through a pipe, convective heat Qh and radiative heat Qr received by Bat2 from the smoke were minimal, at 1713 J and 1780 J, respectively (1.5 % and 1.7 % of the total heat).

claims.claim: The ARC test determined self-heating onset temperature T1 at 96.5°C, thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 at 189.6°C, and maximum temperature T3 at 1086.9°C for the 62 Ah NMC/graphite prismatic cell, with maximum temperature rise rate exceeding 800°C/s.
claims.quote: The self-heating onset temperature T₁, TR trigger temperature T₂, and maximum temperature T₃ were measured at 96.5 ℃, 189.6 ℃, and 1086.9 ℃, respectively, with the maximum TR temperature rise rate (dT/dt) exceeding 800 ℃/s.

claims.claim: LiH exists in the SEI layer of graphite anode and exhibits highly electrochemical reversibility at graphite anode surface during cycling, with HD signal detected for both fully-lithiated (100% SOC) and fully-delithiated (0% SOC) graphite anodes via D2O titration.
claims.quote: It is, the ﬁrst time, discovered that LiH does exist in the SEI layer of graphite anode, and it exhibits highly electrochemical reversibility at graphite anode surface during cycling.

claims.claim: LiH has poor thermal compatibility with the dual-salt electrolyte, with the exothermic reaction starting at approximately 70°C, making it a critical contributor to early triggering of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: LiH was evidenced to have poor thermal compatibility with the dual-salt electrolyte (Figure 4e), with the exothermic reaction starts at ca. 70 °C, which agreed well with the STA results of cycled anode/electrolyte shown in Figure S6 (Supporting Information), implying the critical contribution of LiH/electrolyte exothermic reaction on the early triggering the thermal runaway of the pouch cell.

claims.claim: H2 migrating from anode side to cathode side decreases the thermal stability of cathode/electrolyte, causing Tonset to drop from 136 to 116°C and producing a sharp temperature rise from 159 to 285°C.
claims.quote: Another two-bomb testing reveals that the gas species (especially H2) produced by anode/electrolyte decrease the thermal stability of cathode/electrolyte (Figure 5b). In speciﬁc, Tonset drops from 136 to 116 °C, and a sharp temperature rise from 159 to 285 °C is appeared in the HWS curve.

claims.claim: Phase transformation of lithiated graphite anode and O2-releasing by delithiated NCM523 cathode are accelerating factors for thermal runaway, not the root cause for triggering it.
claims.quote: the LiH induced H2 releasing at anode side and H2 migration to cathode side is the rooted thermal runaway trigger of NCM523/G pouch cell, while the phase transformation of lithiated graphite anode and the O2-releasing by delithiated NCM523 cathode are just accelerating factors for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: A pouch cell without dual-salt electrolyte and a pouch cell with dual-salt electrolyte but without formation process both do not present severe exothermic reactions related to thermal runaway below 250°C, while a cell after one formation cycle at 0% SOC undergoes thermal runaway with Tonset=141°C, Ttr=199°C, Tmax=280°C.
claims.quote: both pouch cells do not present severe exothermic reactions related to the thermal runway of pouch cell below 250 °C (Figure 2d). However, when the 5 Ah NCM523/G pouch cell with dual-salt electrolyte is cycled for one formation cycle (0% SOC), the thermal runaway occurs under the same testing condition in ARC (Tonset = 141 °C, Ttr = 199 °C, Tmax = 280 °C).

claims.claim: Bottom heating mode always results in more severe thermal runaway compared to front heating mode, with higher peak temperature, temperature increment, heat generation, and TR propagation velocity in prismatic LFP batteries at 100% SOC under 500 W heating power.
claims.quote: Compared with the front heating mode, the bottom heating mode always results in severe TR accompanied by higher peak temperature, temperature increment, heat generation, and TR propagation velocity within the battery.

claims.claim: Internal heat generation before thermal runaway contributes more than 35% to inducing the occurrence of TR in prismatic LFP batteries under bottom heating with fixed heating quantity across heating powers of 100–500 W.
claims.quote: The internal heat generation before TR makes a non-negligible contribution to the battery temperature increase and the trigger of TR, accounting for more than 35 %.

claims.claim: Under bottom heating with continuous heating quantity (BH–C), safety venting and thermal runaway occur almost simultaneously, whereas under fixed heating quantity (BH–F) they do not occur simultaneously due to insufficient external heating.
claims.quote: The simultaneous occurrence of SV and TR can be observed in the battery under BH–C, but they don't appear in the mode of BH–F due to insufficient external heating source.

claims.claim: Under fixed heating quantity with bottom heating, thermal runaway is triggered by the decrease of the temperature gradient within the battery rather than by direct external heating.
claims.quote: TR is triggered by the decrease of the temperature gradient within the battery under the limited external heating quantity.

claims.claim: For prismatic LFP batteries under bottom heating, intense exothermic reactions can only be initiated to trigger TR once the temperature in the heating zone exceeds 200°C.
claims.quote: intense exothermic reactions can only be initiated to trigger TR once the temperature is higher than 200°C.

claims.claim: The onset time of TR follows a power-law relationship with heating power (y = ax^b) for prismatic LFP batteries under bottom heating with fixed heating quantity, meaning TR onset time increases exponentially as heating power decreases.
claims.quote: An obvious relationship between the onset time of TR and heating power can be observed, following y = axb. This indicates that the time for the occurrence of SV and TR will increase exponentially with the debasement of heating power.

claims.claim: Under bottom heating at 200 W and 100 W with fixed heating quantity (1575 s heating time), the battery average temperature remained below 200°C (174.5°C and 162.2°C respectively), which was insufficient to trigger TR spontaneously.
claims.quote: the maximum average temperatures of the batteries under 200 W and 100 W are 174.5°C and 162.2°C, respectively. When the battery is exposed to low heating power, the long heating time always results in more heat dissipation, which requires more external heating quantity to trigger the battery TR.

claims.claim: An endothermic process is observed within the battery after safety venting under bottom heating with fixed heating quantity, attributed to separator melting across multiple layers.
claims.quote: a slight drop can be observed in the heat generation curve after the interruption of the external heater, indicating that endothermic reactions such as the separator melting of multiple layers is initiated inside the battery.

claims.claim: The specific heat capacity of the failure LFP battery (post-TR) was determined to be 8.89 J/g·K, compared to approximately 1.1 J/g·K for the normal battery.
claims.quote: the slope in these figures is 8.89, which suggests the specific heat capacity of the failure battery is 8.89 J/g·K. The specific heat capacity of the normal battery is around 1.1 J/g·K.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate and peak temperature under bottom heating with continuous quantity (BH–C) were 5.1°C/s and 428.7°C, respectively, compared to 3.7°C/s and 359.1°C under front heating with continuous quantity (FH-C).
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rise rate and maximum temperature of the battery under FH-C are 3.7°C/s and 359.1°C, respectively, much lower than that of the battery under BH–C with values of 5.1°C/s and 428.7°C.

claims.claim: Lithium plating is the primary failure mechanism of battery sudden death under all four degradation paths (low-temperature cycling, high-rate cycling, high-temperature cycling, and high-temperature calendar aging).
claims.quote: Multi-angle characterization analysis shows that lithium plating is the primary failure mechanism of battery sudden death under different degradation paths.

claims.claim: Under low-temperature cycling (path-L) and high-rate cycling (path-F), the limited lithium intercalation rate in graphite is the primary cause of lithium plating during sudden death.
claims.quote: Under path-L, the kinetic conditions for lithium intercalation into the anode become worse, resulting in delayed intercalation of lithium ions into graphite and ultimately causing lithium plating on the anode surface. Similarly, under path-F, due to the limited rate of lithium intercalation, a substantial amount of lithium ions cannot be swiftly intercalated into the graphite in a short time, resulting in lithium plating on the anode surface. In both cases, the limited lithium intercalation rate is the primary cause for lithium plating.

claims.claim: Under high-temperature cycling (path-H) and high-temperature calendar aging (path-R), lithium plating is caused by localized anode drying and uneven potential distribution resulting from gas generation between electrodes.
claims.quote: Under high-temperature conditions, gases generated exists between the electrodes, obstructing the lithium intercalation sites... Furthermore, the gas changes the distance between the electrodes and consumes the electrolyte, leading to localized drying of the anode. Changes in electrode spacing and localized drying result in an uneven distribution of the anode potential, ultimately causing lithium plating upon recharging.

claims.claim: After sudden death, the self-heating initial temperature (T1) decreases from an initial 115.7°C to 53.3°C, 53.9°C, 63.9°C, and 59.4°C under path-L, path-F, path-H, and path-R respectively, with path-L and path-F showing more severe decline than path-H and path-R.
claims.quote: the T1 of the cell after sudden death decreases from initial state of 115.7°C to 53.3°C, 53.9°C, 63.9°C, and 59.4°C under the path-L, path-F, path-H, and path-R, respectively.

claims.claim: Sudden death primarily affects the anode interface polarization process under path-L and path-F, causing the IC curve peak to shift toward higher voltage, whereas under path-H and path-R it primarily affects the charge transfer process, causing the IC curve peak to shift toward lower voltage.
claims.quote: Sudden death under path-L and path-F primarily affects the polarization process at the anode interface, leading to increased lithium stripping and deposition, and changes the anode potential, resulting in a significant shift of the peak of IC curve towards higher voltage. However, sudden death under path-H and path-R mainly affects the charge transfer process... causing the peak of IC curve to shift significantly towards lower voltage.

claims.claim: Under path-H and path-R, the charge transfer process evolves from a solid-liquid two-phase system to a solid-liquid-gas three-phase system due to gas generation, substantially increasing charge transfer resistance after sudden death.
claims.quote: The primary cause for this phenomenon is the gas generation within the cell under high-temperature conditions. The charge transfer process evolves from a solid-liquid two-phase system to a solid-liquid-gas three-phase system. Specifically, the charge transfer process between the gas and solid phases corresponding to τ′3 peak encounters substantial resistance, leading to a rapid increase in the area corresponding to τ′3 peak after the cell sudden death.

claims.claim: The thermal stability reduction of the SEI film after sudden death is notably more severe under path-L and path-F compared to path-H and path-R, because at high temperatures plated lithium rapidly reacts with electrolyte to form metastable side reaction products.
claims.quote: The extent of the thermal stability reduction in the SEI film is notably more severe in path-L and path-F compared to path-H and path-R... at high temperature, the plated lithium rapidly reacts with the electrolyte, forming metastable side reaction products. Consequently, the thermal stability of the anode after sudden death under path-H and path-R is marginally higher compared to that under path-L and path-F.

claims.claim: During nail penetration testing, the self-polymerized SEI on the cathode was triggered at 110–113 °C in the 2–3 s period, causing voltage and temperature to increase as the nail continued penetrating.
claims.quote: the primary self-polymerized SEI on the cathode was triggered at 110–113 ◦C in the 2–3 s period to increase voltage and temperature as the nail continued its penetration

claims.claim: In the nail penetration test, voltage fell quickly to approximately 0.5 V and temperature increased to 110 °C during the first 1–2 s after nail penetration at 1 mm/s.
claims.quote: As the nail penetrated at a slow speed of 1 mm/s, the voltage fell quickly to approximately 0.5 V, and the temperature increased to 110 ◦C during the ﬁrst 1–2 s.

claims.claim: After complete nail penetration (27–100 s stage), the battery with additive protection remained at approximately 100 °C without producing any smoke or explosion.
claims.quote: the voltage of the battery quickly dropped to 0 V, and the temperature of the battery with additive protection remained at approximately 100 ◦C, without producing any smoke or explosion.

claims.claim: Adding 1.5 wt% branched oligomer additive reduced the free volume of the battery from 145.3 Å³ to 130.4 Å³, a decrease of 15 Å³ corresponding to a 0.22% reduction in free volume fraction.
claims.quote: The free volume of a battery without any additive is 145.3 ˚A3, but this suddenly decreases to 130.4 ˚A3 with the addition of 1.5% additive. This behavior indicates that the 1.5% additive in the battery formed a dense SEI and occupied 15 ˚A3 of free volume. This 0.22% decrease in free volume conﬁrms a self-polymerized electrochemical reaction in the formation of SEI.

claims.claim: The composite electrode with additive had a slight effect of approximately 3.6% on discharge capacity at 1 C rate in a full cell, while charge capacity and OCP were not affected.
claims.quote: it had a slight effect of approximately 3.6% on discharge capacity. In a regular battery system, a slight amount of SEI on the cathode may not affect ionic transformation. However, the lithium ions de-intercalated from the anode and intercalated to the cathode when they encountered resistance in the thick cathode SEI.

claims.claim: A critical temperature threshold of approximately 135°C separates two opposite thermal stability patterns in LiCoO2 pouch cells aged under high-temperature float charge (HTFC) conditions.
claims.quote: A novel finding of this study is the identification of a critical temperature threshold (~135°C) that separates two opposite thermal stability patterns.

claims.claim: Below 135°C, thermal stability of HTFC-aged LIBs increases with decreasing state of health (SOH), primarily due to decomposition and regeneration of the SEI.
claims.quote: Below this threshold, thermal stability increases with decreasing state of health (SOH), primarily due to decomposition and regeneration of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI).

claims.claim: Above 135°C, thermal stability of HTFC-aged LIBs decreases with decreasing SOH because of separator aging and cathode degradation, which substantially raise the risk of internal short circuits and thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Above the threshold, thermal stability decreases with decreasing SOH because of separator aging and cathode degradation, which substantially raise the risk of internal short circuits and thermal runaway (TR).

claims.claim: The gases generated during HTFC aging, mainly CO2 and light hydrocarbons, have a calculated lower flammability limit (LFL) of approximately 27.94 vol%, indicating they are flammable but not easily ignitable, with relatively low explosion risk.
claims.quote: The LFL is calculated to be approximately 27.94 vol%... It is evident that the LFL of the internal mixed gases exceeds 20%, indicating that while the gases are flammable, they are not easily ignitable, and the associated safety risk is relatively low.

claims.claim: At SOH of 80%, the onset temperature of self-heating (Tonset) increases by 25.5°C compared to the fresh cell, while TISC and TTR decrease by 25.1°C and 12.6°C respectively.
claims.quote: for the SOH of 80%, Tonset increases by 25.5℃ compared to the fresh cell... When the SOH reaches 80%, TISC and TTR decrease by 25.1℃ and 12.6℃, respectively, compared to the fresh cell.

claims.claim: In Stage I (Tonset to 135°C), activation energy Ea and frequency factor lgA both increase linearly with decreasing SOH during HTFC aging, indicating the exothermic reaction becomes harder to trigger but faster once initiated.
claims.quote: for Stage I, both Ea and lgA increase linearly with the decrease in SOH... the exothermic reaction in Stage I becomes more difficult to trigger as the battery ages, but once triggered, the reaction rate increases.

claims.claim: Reaction enthalpy H of the self-heating process decreases linearly as SOH decreases, due to reduction in energy storage capacity of the battery with aging.
claims.quote: Fig. 9(c) indicates that the energy released by the battery's self-heating decreases linearly as SOH decreases with aging, due to the reduction in the energy storage capacity of the battery as it ages.

claims.claim: The time difference between TISC and TTR (△tISC) increases progressively with aging, indicating that aged batteries sustain internal short circuits for longer durations before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: with the increasing aging of the battery, the temperature at which TISC decreases, and the time difference (△t) between TISC and TTR gradually increases.

claims.claim: Anode degradation, not cathode degradation, plays the dominant role in capacity fade during HTFC aging, as indicated by the rapid decline and eventual disappearance of IC peaks (1) and (2) while peaks (3) and (4) stabilize.
claims.quote: Peaks (1) and (2) decline rapidly and eventually disappear, while peaks (3) and (4) tend to stabilize, indicating that anode degradation plays a dominant role in the HTFC aging process.

claims.claim: EDS mapping revealed the presence of significant Co on the anode surface after HTFC aging, indicating cathode disproportionation causing Co dissolution and migration to the anode.
claims.quote: EDS mapping (Fig. 4(b)) reveals the presence of a significant amount of Co on the anode surface after HTFC aging, indicating that the cathode undergoes a disproportionation reaction, causing some Co to dissolve and transition to the anode surface.

claims.claim: In low-temperature cycled (0°C) aged lithium-ion batteries, the reaction between deposited lithium and the electrolyte accounts for more than 50% of the total side-reaction heat during overcharge-induced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the heat produced by the reaction between deposited lithium and the electrolyte in low-temperature aged batteries is 1372 J and 1467 J, accounting for more than 50 % of the total side-reaction heat.

claims.claim: In high-temperature cycled (55°C) aged lithium-ion batteries, the reaction between deposited lithium and the electrolyte contributes less than 30% of the total side-reaction heat during overcharge-induced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: in high-temperature aged batteries, the corresponding values are 676.8 J and 321.4 J, contributing less than 30 % to the total side-reaction heat.

claims.claim: SEI film thickening in high-temperature aged batteries suppresses lithium-ion transport and reduces the reaction rate between deposited lithium and the electrolyte, resulting in lower peak TR temperature and delayed time to peak temperature.
claims.quote: for high-temperature aged batteries, between 110°C and 130°C, QLi decreases with increasing aging due to SEI thickening, which hinders lithium transport and suppresses lithium-electrolyte reactions. Consequently, high-temperature aged batteries exhibit improved thermal stability, characterized by lower TMAX and delayed tMAX.

claims.claim: Low-temperature aged batteries (LTC-80% SOH) reach TR trigger temperature 57 seconds earlier than high-temperature aged batteries at the same SOH during overcharge.
claims.quote: the valve-opening times of low-temperature aged batteries at 90 % and 80 % SOH occur 22 s and 57 s earlier than high-temperature aged batteries, respectively.

claims.claim: Lithium deposition on the anode surface occurs only when the anode potential drops below 0 V versus lithium reference.
claims.quote: Lithium deposition on the anode surface occurs only when the anode potential is lower than 0 V.

claims.claim: Large-scale internal short-circuiting contributes approximately 60% of total heat generation during thermal runaway across all aging paths, with minimal variation between aging conditions.
claims.quote: the majority of the heat during TR originates from large-scale internal short-circuiting, contributing approximately 60 % across all aging paths, showing minimal variation.

claims.claim: For LTC-aged batteries, capacity loss is assumed to arise entirely from lithium plating, and the initial dimensionless concentration of deposited lithium is quantified as cLi,0 = 1 − SOH.
claims.quote: For batteries aged under LTC, the primary aging mechanism is lithium plating on the anode. Therefore, it is assumed that capacity loss is entirely due to lithium plating... The initial dimensionless concentration of lithium plating (cLi,0) can be expressed as follows by Eq. (17). cLi,0 = 1 − C/C0 = 1 − SOH

claims.claim: High-temperature cycling (55°C) results in significant increases in ohmic resistance, SEI film resistance, and charge transfer resistance, primarily due to high temperatures accelerating side reactions and thickening the SEI film.
claims.quote: HTC results in significant increases in ohmic resistance (Rohm), SEI film resistance (Rsei), and charge transfer resistance (Rct). This is primarily due to high temperatures accelerating various side reactions during charge and discharge cycles, leading to the thickening of the SEI film and hindering lithium-ion transport.

claims.claim: Low-temperature aged batteries (LTC-90% SOH and LTC-80% SOH) exhibit temperature rises of 65.96°C and 71.94°C respectively during the overcharge period from VLP to VCR, compared to 36.2°C and 37.6°C for high-temperature aged batteries at the same SOH levels.
claims.quote: During the period from VLP to VCR, the temperature change for low-temperature aged batteries (LTC-90 % SOH and LTC-80 % SOH) was 65.96°C and 71.94°C, while for high-temperature aged batteries (HTC-90 % SOH and HTC-80 % SOH), the temperature change was 36.2°C and 37.6°C.

claims.claim: Under 1C charging conditions, thermal runaway occurs at 659 s, which is 33 s earlier than the 692 s under the uncharged condition.
claims.quote: under charging conditions, the thermal runaway occurs in 659 s, which is 33 s earlier than the 692 s under the uncharged condition

claims.claim: The electrochemical heat production at 3C discharge (1528.89 J) is much higher than at 1C discharge (661.58 J), and both are higher than their corresponding charge conditions at the same rate.
claims.quote: The discharge process generates more electrochemical heat than the charge process, for example, 1528.89J at 3C discharge, is much higher than that at the same rate charge condition

claims.claim: At 1C discharge, electrochemical heat production (661.58 J) is approximately 15% higher than at 1C charging, accelerating the initial decomposition of the SEI layer.
claims.quote: In the case of 1C discharge, for example, the electrochemical heat production (661.58 J) is about 15 % higher than charging, accelerating the initial decomposition of the SEI layer

claims.claim: The thermal runaway trigger time under 5C discharge conditions is 106 s shorter than that under 1C discharge conditions.
claims.quote: The simulation results show that the thermal runaway trigger time under 5C discharge conditions is 106 s shorter than that of 1C

claims.claim: Under identical external heating conditions, the advancement of thermal runaway increases linearly with charge/discharge rate, with discharge operations accelerating thermal runaway by 24% more than charging operations.
claims.quote: the acceleration of thermal runaway is linearly related to the rate, with discharge operations accelerating it by 24 % compared to charging operations

claims.claim: SEI layers generally decompose exothermically between 90–120°C, and after SEI decomposition, lithium ions of the cathode react with the electrolyte at trigger temperatures higher than 120°C, while electrolyte decomposition occurs above 200°C.
claims.quote: SEI layers generally decompose exothermically between 90–120 ◦C. After the SEI layer decomposes, the lithium ions of the cathode react with the electrolyte with a trigger temperature higher than 120◦C... Electrolytes decomposition at temperatures above 200◦C

claims.claim: At 5C discharge, the peak temperature during thermal runaway is lower than at 1C and 3C discharge because rapid discharge consumes stored electrochemical energy before thermal runaway, resulting in less heat generated during the runaway event.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of 5C discharge condition is lower than the other discharge rates, which is due to the rapid discharge consuming the stored electrochemical energy, and the heat generated on the thermal runaway is less than in the 1C and 3C discharge conditions

claims.claim: Removing EC from the electrolyte improved the thermal runaway triggering temperature from 193.1°C to 260.1°C in NCM811 cells.
claims.quote: Simply eliminating EC from the electrolyte is able to improve the thermal runaway triggering temperature from 193.1◦C to 260.1◦C

claims.claim: Reducing EC content from 30% to 15% causes up to 26.9% conductivity loss in the electrolyte.
claims.quote: Studies by Yaakov et al. have underscored the substantial conductivity loss—up to 26.9 %—upon reducing EC content from 30 % to 15 %
